Proof. That $Q_{\theta}^{*}$ is in the class $\mathcal{M}$ follows from the Markov nature of $\mathcal{H}$... The value of $C$ follows from the fact that events must occur at total rate $k(k-1+\theta) / 2$ when there are $k$ lineages in the ancestry (see Stephens (2000)).
comments.quote:
The constant of proportionality $C$ is given by

$$
C=\frac{n(n-1+\theta)}{2},
$$

where $n$ is the number of chromosomes in $H_{i}$.
Proof. That $Q_{\theta}^{*}$ is in the class $\mathcal{M}$ follows from the Markov nature of $\mathcal{H}$... The value of $C$ follows from the fact that events must occur at total rate $k(k-1+\theta) / 2$ when there are $k$ lineages in the ancestry (see Stephens (2000)).
comments.message: At first the sentence "The value of $C$ follows from the fact that events must occur at total rate $k(k-1+\theta)/2$ when there are $k$ lineages in the ancestry (see Stephens (2000))" made me think that more justification was needed, since the normalisation of the backward transition probabilities in (15) is not shown by direct summation. Then I understood that this step is simply using the standard fact for time-reversed continuous-time Markov chains observed at stationarity: the total exit rate from a state is unchanged under time reversal, so given that events in the (forward) ancestry occur at rate $k(k-1+\theta)/2$ when there are $k$ lineages, the same constant must normalise the backward rates. You might consider adding a brief remark or pointer that makes this Markov-process argument explicit, as readers who do not immediately recall this general result could pause at this point in the proof of Theorem 1.

comments.title: Possible notational inconsistency in proof of Theorem 1
comments.paragraph:
P\left\{\Upsilon_{\mathrm{m}} \cap A_{k}(t-\delta)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \beta\right) \mid A_{k}(t)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \alpha\right)\right\} & \\
\quad= & \frac{P\left\{\Upsilon_{\mathrm{m}} \cap A_{k}(t-\delta)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \beta\right) \cap A_{k}(t)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \alpha\right)\right\}}{P\left\{A_{k}(t)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \alpha\right)\right\}} \\
& =\frac{\pi\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{n-1}, \beta\right) \delta \theta P_{\beta \alpha} / 2}{\pi\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{n-1}, \alpha\right)}+o(\delta)
comments.quote:
P\left\{\Upsilon_{\mathrm{m}} \cap A_{k}(t-\delta)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \beta\right) \mid A_{k}(t)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \alpha\right)\right\} & \\
\quad= & \frac{P\left\{\Upsilon_{\mathrm{m}} \cap A_{k}(t-\delta)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \beta\right) \cap A_{k}(t)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \alpha\right)\right\}}{P\left\{A_{k}(t)=\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{k-1}, \alpha\right)\right\}} \\
& =\frac{\pi\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{n-1}, \beta\right) \delta \theta P_{\beta \alpha} / 2}{\pi\left(\alpha_{1}, \ldots, \alpha_{n-1}, \alpha\right)}+o(\delta)
comments.message: At first the appearance of $\pi(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_{n-1},\beta)$ and $\pi(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_{n-1},\alpha)$ in the middle line of this display made me think that the stationary distribution was being evaluated on an $n$-dimensional argument, even though the surrounding setup is explicitly for $k$ ancestral lineages with $A_k(t)=(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_{k-1},\alpha)$. Then I understood from the preceding and following lines, and from the rewrite in terms of $\pi(\beta\mid A_k-\alpha)$ and $\pi(\alpha\mid A_k-\alpha)$, that these factors must correspond to $\pi(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_{k-1},\beta)$ and $\pi(\alpha_1,\ldots,\alpha_{k-1},\alpha)$ and that the use of $n-1$ here is simply an indexing slip. It might nevertheless be helpful to correct these subscripts (and, if you wish, to note that $\pi$ is being used generically for the appropriate $k$-dimensional marginal of the stationary distribution) so that the dimension of the state space is completely consistent within the proof.

comments.title: Interpretation of the transition matrix in Proposition 1(e)
comments.paragraph:
The distribution $\hat{\pi}\left(\cdot \mid A_{n}\right)$ is the stationary distribution of the Markov chain on $E$ with transition matrix

$$
T_{\beta \alpha}=\frac{\theta}{n+\theta} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n+\theta},
$$

i.e. ... Remark 1. ... Property (e) provides a characterization of $\pi$ which is, in some more general settings not considered in this paper, more amenable to generalization than definition 1. The Markov transition matrix (21) is the transition matrix associated with the $(n+1)$ th line of the particle representation described in Section 4 (or the Moran model) when the first $n$ lines are fixed to be $A_{n}$.
comments.quote:
The distribution $\hat{\pi}\left(\cdot \mid A_{n}\right)$ is the stationary distribution of the Markov chain on $E$ with transition matrix

$$
T_{\beta \alpha}=\frac{\theta}{n+\theta} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n+\theta},
$$

i.e. ... Remark 1. ... Property (e) provides a characterization of $\pi$ which is, in some more general settings not considered in this paper, more amenable to generalization than definition 1. The Markov transition matrix (21) is the transition matrix associated with the $(n+1)$ th line of the particle representation described in Section 4 (or the Moran model) when the first $n$ lines are fixed to be $A_{n}$.
comments.message:
The identification in Remark 1 of the matrix in equation (21) with "the transition matrix associated with the $(n+1)$th line of the particle representation … when the first $n$ lines are fixed to be $A_n$" appears to be too strong if taken literally. Under the particle representation in Section 4, a single lineage mutates at rate $\theta/2$ and copies from the $n$ fixed lines at total rate $n$, so the embedded jump chain for that lineage has transition probabilities
\[
T'_{\beta\alpha} = \frac{(\theta/2)P_{\beta\alpha} + n_\alpha}{\theta/2 + n},
\]
whereas the matrix (21) is
\[
T_{\beta\alpha} = \frac{\theta}{n+\theta}P_{\beta\alpha} + \frac{n_\alpha}{n+\theta}.
\]
These kernels are not identical for general $n$ and $\theta$; in particular, they differ by a factor of 2 in the relative weight of mutation versus copying events. The algebraic statement of Proposition 1(e) that $\hat\pi(\cdot\mid A_n)$ is the stationary distribution of $T$ is entirely correct, but the genealogical interpretation in Remark 1 seems to conflate this auxiliary chain with the exact jump chain of the $(n+1)$th line in the specified particle representation. It would be helpful to adjust the wording to clarify that (21) corresponds to a Markov chain that mixes mutation and copying steps in proportions governed by $\theta$ and $n$, rather than to the literal embedded chain of the particle representation with mutation rate $\theta/2$.

comments.title: Unclear step in the proof of Proposition 2
comments.paragraph:
Thus

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} & =\sum_{\beta \in E} \frac{\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}\left(\frac{\theta}{n-1+\theta} \frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} \frac{n_{\alpha}-1}{n-1+\theta}\right) \\
& =\sum_{\beta \in E} \frac{\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)} C^{-1} \frac{\theta}{2} n_{\alpha} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{1}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)} C^{-1}\binom{n_{\alpha}}{2} \\
& =p_{\mathrm{m}}(\alpha)+p_{\mathrm{c}}(\alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$
comments.quote:
Thus

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} & =\sum_{\beta \in E} \frac{\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}\left(\frac{\theta}{n-1+\theta} \frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} \frac{n_{\alpha}-1}{n-1+\theta}\right) \\
& =\sum_{\beta \in E} \frac{\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)} C^{-1} \frac{\theta}{2} n_{\alpha} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{1}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)} C^{-1}\binom{n_{\alpha}}{2} \\
& =p_{\mathrm{m}}(\alpha)+p_{\mathrm{c}}(\alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$
comments.message:
At first this step in the proof of Proposition 2 made me think that the authors were simply replacing the summand
\[
\frac{\theta}{n-1+\theta} \frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} \frac{n_{\alpha}-1}{n-1+\theta}
\]
by the two terms in the next line, which would not hold termwise. Then I understood that the first part is just rewritten using $C^{-1}=2/\{n(n-1+\theta)\}$, and that the second part has been summed over $\beta$ using $\sum_{\beta}\hat{\pi}(\beta\mid H_i-\alpha)=1$, giving the term involving $C^{-1}\binom{n_\alpha}{2}$. It might help the reader if this use of the normalisation of $\hat{\pi}(\cdot\mid\cdot)$ and the separation of the two contributions (the sum and the already-summed term) were mentioned explicitly at this point.

comments.title: Use of a further approximation for $\hat{\pi}$ in applications
comments.paragraph: Note that this model has $2^{10}$ different alleles, and so the calculation of the quantities $\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid A_{n}\right)$ using equations (18) and (19) appears to be computationally daunting. In fact the special structure of this model allows an efficient approximation of these quantities, as described in Appendix A.
comments.quote: Note that this model has $2^{10}$ different alleles, and so the calculation of the quantities $\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid A_{n}\right)$ using equations (18) and (19) appears to be computationally daunting. In fact the special structure of this model allows an efficient approximation of these quantities, as described in Appendix A.
comments.message: I initially had trouble seeing exactly what version of $\hat{\pi}(\cdot\mid\cdot)$ was used in the simulated sequence example, because the main text only refers to an "efficient approximation" and then moves on to the numerical results. However, I later saw that Appendix A makes clear that a numerical quadrature scheme is used to approximate the expression for $\hat{\pi}$ from Definition 1, and that the resulting proposal is a valid IS function in its own right. To make the link between Sections 4 and 5.2 entirely explicit, it might be helpful to state directly in Section 5.2 that the performance reported there for $Q_\theta^{\mathrm{SD}}$ is based on this practical numerical approximation to $\hat{\pi}$ as described in Appendix A.

comments.title: Inverted description of microsatellite boundary conditions
comments.paragraph: The implementation of our IS scheme is facilitated by centring the sample distribution near 10 repeats and truncating the type space $E$ to $\{0,1, \ldots, 19\}$ by insisting that all mutations to alleles of length 0 or 19 involve the gain or loss respectively of a single repeat.
comments.quote: The implementation of our IS scheme is facilitated by centring the sample distribution near 10 repeats and truncating the type space $E$ to $\{0,1, \ldots, 19\}$ by insisting that all mutations to alleles of length 0 or 19 involve the gain or loss respectively of a single repeat.
comments.message:
The sentence

"The implementation of our IS scheme is facilitated by centring the sample distribution near 10 repeats and truncating the type space $E$ to $\{0,1, \ldots, 19\}$ by insisting that all mutations to alleles of length 0 or 19 involve the gain or loss respectively of a single repeat."

appears to have the gain/loss description at the boundaries reversed relative to the stepwise model defined just above. Under that model, the only forward-time mutation yielding allele 0 is 1→0, which is a loss of a repeat, and the only mutation yielding 19 is 18→19, which is a gain. As written, "gain or loss respectively" suggests the opposite mapping. This is very likely just an inversion in wording (for example, "loss or gain" or "from" instead of "to" would match the usual reflecting-boundary convention), and since the observed alleles are far from 0 and 19 the precise boundary behaviour has negligible effect on the examples. Nonetheless, it would be helpful to correct or clarify this sentence so that the intended direction of gain and loss at the lower and upper boundaries is unambiguous for readers wishing to reproduce the implementation.

comments.title: Justification for the infinite sites proposal distribution
comments.paragraph: These problems are not insurmountable, but for simplicity we adapt our earlier approach to this context by analogy with proposition 2: recall that one method of simulating from our IS function $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ begins by choosing a chromosome uniformly at random from those present and assuming that this chromosome is involved in the most recent event backwards in time. ... Analogy with proposition 2 then suggests choosing the most recent event backwards in time by drawing a chromosome uniformly at random from those satisfying either (a) or (b). This procedure defines an IS function $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ which we note is independent of $\theta$, removing the need to specify a driving value.
comments.quote: These problems are not insurmountable, but for simplicity we adapt our earlier approach to this context by analogy with proposition 2: recall that one method of simulating from our IS function $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ begins by choosing a chromosome uniformly at random from those present and assuming that this chromosome is involved in the most recent event backwards in time. ... Analogy with proposition 2 then suggests choosing the most recent event backwards in time by drawing a chromosome uniformly at random from those satisfying either (a) or (b). This procedure defines an IS function $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ which we note is independent of $\theta$, removing the need to specify a driving value.
comments.message:
Statement "for simplicity we adapt our earlier approach to this context by analogy with proposition 2 … This procedure defines an IS function $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ which we note is independent of $\theta$" initially made me wonder how closely the infinite‑sites sampler is tied to the general construction of $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ via Theorem 1 and Definition 2. The text does indicate that, because of technical complications (uncountable type space, non‑reversibility), you are now using a more heuristic, analogy‑based design: uniform choice over the chromosomes that could have been involved in the last event, rather than an explicit approximation to $Q^*_\theta$ built from a surrogate $\hat\pi$.

However, since $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ was earlier defined quite precisely as the θ‑dependent sampler obtained by substituting $\hat\pi$ into Theorem 1, reusing the symbol for this θ‑independent, heuristic infinite‑sites proposal may cause some readers to momentarily conflate the two constructions and to overestimate how directly Section 4's theory applies here.

It might therefore be helpful to make the nature of this extension more explicit: to state clearly that in the infinite‑sites case you are no longer working within the Theorem 1/Definition 2 framework but are instead introducing a separate, genealogically motivated proposal that happens to share the same notation; and perhaps to add a short remark on why you expect this uniform‑over‑eligible‑chromosomes choice to be a reasonable approximation in practice. This would clarify the relationship between the main theoretical development and the infinite‑sites special case without altering the substance of the method.

comments.title: Clarity of the rooted tree sampler modification (Sec 5.5)
comments.paragraph: To facilitate a comparison with published estimates, we modified our IS function to analyse rooted trees, by adding to conditions (a) and (b) above a condition that no mutation can occur backwards in time from the type of the MRCA. Note that this does not take full account of the information contained in the position of the root, and the modified sampler is likely to be less efficient for roots which are relatively unlikely given the data.
comments.quote: To facilitate a comparison with published estimates, we modified our IS function to analyse rooted trees, by adding to conditions (a) and (b) above a condition that no mutation can occur backwards in time from the type of the MRCA. Note that this does not take full account of the information contained in the position of the root, and the modified sampler is likely to be less efficient for roots which are relatively unlikely given the data.
comments.message:
Statement "we modified our IS function to analyse rooted trees … by adding … a condition that no mutation can occur backwards in time from the type of the MRCA" caused me some initial uncertainty about exactly how the backward moves were constrained for the rooted case. At first it was not obvious whether "from the type of the MRCA" referred to forbidding proposals that originate on lineages of the designated MRCA type, or to some other restriction, and one also has to keep in mind that in the rooted–tree setting a particular MRCA type has been fixed in advance.

After reading the surrounding discussion on rooted trees and outgroup information, it becomes clear that the intent is to condition on a chosen root type and then forbid any backward mutation steps that originate from a lineage currently carrying that root type, while still allowing coalescences between such lineages. This ensures that the proposal is restricted to histories in which the designated root type is ancestral rather than created by mutation.

Because this is the only change needed to adapt the unrooted sampler to the rooted setting, it may be worth stating explicitly that the algorithm conditions on a particular MRCA type and then disallows backward mutation moves that originate on lineages of that type. This would make the nature of the additional constraint fully transparent to readers implementing the rooted sampler.

comments.title: Unclear description of ascertainment correction
comments.paragraph: For the IS schemes that we have considered, assuming the infinite sites mutation model, the ascertainment effect can be accommodated by labelling every lineage which leads to any chromosome in the panel as a panel lineage and adapting both $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ and $Q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ so that mutations can occur only on panel lineages.
comments.quote: For the IS schemes that we have considered, assuming the infinite sites mutation model, the ascertainment effect can be accommodated by labelling every lineage which leads to any chromosome in the panel as a panel lineage and adapting both $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ and $Q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ so that mutations can occur only on panel lineages.
comments.message:
The description of the SNP ascertainment correction in this paragraph is rather compressed, and the role of the "panel lineage" labelling could be clearer. As written, labelling "every lineage which leads to any chromosome in the panel" as a panel lineage and then allowing mutations "only on panel lineages" would in principle include branches ancestral to all panel members as well as those ancestral to a proper subset. For the two‑stage design you describe, the ascertained sites are specifically those that are polymorphic within the panel, so the mutation for any ascertained SNP must fall on a branch that partitions the panel, not on the trunk ancestral to the entire panel.

In the full inferential framework, consistency with the observed polymorphism in the panel will be enforced via the likelihood factor $\pi_\theta(A_n \mid \mathcal{H})$, and mutations on branches that make the panel monomorphic will automatically be given zero weight. However, the current wording does not spell out this interaction and could be read as if restricting mutations to panel lineages alone were sufficient to encode the ascertainment scheme. It would help readers if you could make explicit how the event "SNP is polymorphic in the panel" is imposed in your IS formulation, and in particular distinguish between branches ancestral to all panel members and those that genuinely partition the panel.

comments.title: Proposed extension for long sequences is unclear
comments.paragraph: The effect of the non-varying sites could then be taken into account by the factor $\pi_{\theta}\left(A_{n} \mid \mathcal{H}^{(i)}\right)$ in estimator (9), which could be calculated by the peeling algorithm (to do this it would be necessary to apply IS to the full typed ancestry $\mathcal{A}$ of the sample).
comments.quote: The effect of the non-varying sites could then be taken into account by the factor $\pi_{\theta}\left(A_{n} \mid \mathcal{H}^{(i)}\right)$ in estimator (9), which could be calculated by the peeling algorithm (to do this it would be necessary to apply IS to the full typed ancestry $\mathcal{A}$ of the sample).
comments.message:
The brief discussion of long sequences in Section 6.2 is difficult to interpret in light of the earlier definitions of $\mathcal{H}$, $\mathcal{A}$ and $\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid \cdot)$. Earlier in the paper, $\mathcal{H}$ is defined as a history without times or topology, and equation (2) defines $\pi_\theta(A_n \mid \mathcal{H})$ as a purely combinatorial term depending only on $H_0$, independent of $\theta$ and of any tree structure. In contrast, the peeling algorithm takes as input a fully specified genealogy with branch lengths (part of $\mathcal{A}$) and computes a sequence likelihood $P_\theta(A_n \mid \mathcal{G})$.

In the quoted sentence you suggest that "the effect of the non‑varying sites could then be taken into account by the factor $\pi_\theta(A_n \mid \mathcal{H}^{(i)})$ … which could be calculated by the peeling algorithm," with a parenthetical remark that this would require applying IS to the full typed ancestry $\mathcal{A}$. As it stands, this mixes two different notions: the earlier $\pi_\theta(A_n \mid \mathcal{H})$ cannot be evaluated by peeling, and peeling cannot be applied at the level of $\mathcal{H}$ alone. The parenthesis hints at a switch of missing data from $\mathcal{H}$ to $\mathcal{A}$ for this extension, but that switch and the associated change in the meaning of the likelihood factor are not made explicit.

For readers interested in implementing such an extension, it would be useful to clarify that the peeling algorithm would be used to compute a full sequence likelihood $P_\theta(A_n \mid \mathcal{A}^{(i)})$ (or the contribution of the non‑varying sites) conditional on a sampled typed ancestry, and that in this context IS would operate on $\mathcal{A}$ rather than on histories $\mathcal{H}$ alone.

comments.title: Formulation of the Griffiths-Tavaré estimator
comments.paragraph:
In the Griffiths-Tavaré method, view the problem of finding $\{p(\mathbf{n})\}$ as solving the system of linear
equations (34). Importance sampling techniques were used to obtain a solution of the equations. Rescale $P\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)$ and interpret as a probability distribution

$$
q\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime} \mid H_{j}\right)=p\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right) / f\left(H_{j}\right)
$$

where $f\left(H_{j}\right)=\Sigma p\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)$. Then

$$
p\left(H_{j}\right)=f\left(H_{j}\right) \sum q\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime} \mid H_{j}\right) p\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right),
$$

leading to an importance sampling representation

$$
p\left(H_{0}\right)=E_{q}\left\{f\left(H_{0}\right) f\left(H_{-1}\right) \ldots f\left(H_{-m}\right)\right\}
$$

with absorption at $-m$, where there first is a single ancestor of the sample. The proposal distribution for importance sampling is $q(\cdot \mid \cdot)$.
comments.quote:
In the Griffiths-Tavaré method, view the problem of finding $\{p(\mathbf{n})\}$ as solving the system of linear
equations (34). Importance sampling techniques were used to obtain a solution of the equations. Rescale $P\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)$ and interpret as a probability distribution

$$
q\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime} \mid H_{j}\right)=p\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right) / f\left(H_{j}\right)
$$

where $f\left(H_{j}\right)=\Sigma p\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)$. Then

$$
p\left(H_{j}\right)=f\left(H_{j}\right) \sum q\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime} \mid H_{j}\right) p\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right),
$$

leading to an importance sampling representation

$$
p\left(H_{0}\right)=E_{q}\left\{f\left(H_{0}\right) f\left(H_{-1}\right) \ldots f\left(H_{-m}\right)\right\}
$$

with absorption at $-m$, where there first is a single ancestor of the sample. The proposal distribution for importance sampling is $q(\cdot \mid \cdot)$.
comments.message:
In the displayed importance sampling representation for the Griffiths–Tavaré method,
\[
p(H_0)=E_q\{f(H_0) f(H_{-1}) \ldots f(H_{-m})\},
\]
I found it difficult to reconcile the indexing with the recursion that precedes it. From
\[
p(H_j)=\sum_{H_{j-1}'} P(H_j\mid H_{j-1}')\,p(H_{j-1}')=f(H_j)\sum_{H_{j-1}'} q(H_{j-1}'\mid H_j)\,p(H_{j-1}'),
\]
iterating backwards until the first single-ancestor state \(H_{-m}\) leads to
\[
p(H_0)=E_q\Big\{p(H_{-m})\,f(H_0) f(H_{-1}) \cdots f(H_{-(m-1)})\Big\}.
\]
That is, the product over the normalising factors \(f(H_j)\) stops at the last non-absorbing configuration, and the terminal factor should be the boundary probability \(p(H_{-m})\) of the ancestral state. As written, the expression appears to include an extra factor \(f(H_{-m})\), which is not defined by \(f(H_j)=\sum_{H_{j-1}'} P(H_j\mid H_{j-1}')\) at the absorbing single-ancestor state. Clarifying that the final factor is \(p(H_{-m})\) (or that \(f(H_{-m})\) is being used as shorthand for this boundary term) would make the connection between the linear system (34) and the importance sampling representation precise.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation in discussion of methods
comments.paragraph:
In the Stephens-Donnelly method,

$$
p\left(H_{j}\right)=\sum \frac{p\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)}{\hat{p}\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime} \mid H_{j}\right)} \hat{p}\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime} \mid H_{j}\right) p\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)
$$

The importance sampling representation is

$$
\begin{aligned}
p\left(H_{0}\right) & =E_{\hat{p}}\left\{\frac{p\left(H_{0} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{\hat{p}\left(H_{-1} \mid H_{0}\right)} \cdots \frac{p\left(H_{-m+1} \mid H_{-m}\right)}{\hat{p}\left(H_{-m} \mid H_{-m+1}\right)} p\left(H_{-m}\right)\right\} \\
& =E_{\hat{p}}\left\{\frac{p\left(\mathcal{H}_{\rightarrow}\right)}{\hat{p}\left(\mathcal{H}_{\leftarrow}\right)}\right\} .
\end{aligned}
$$
comments.quote:
In the Stephens-Donnelly method,

$$
p\left(H_{j}\right)=\sum \frac{p\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)}{\hat{p}\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime} \mid H_{j}\right)} \hat{p}\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime} \mid H_{j}\right) p\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)
$$

The importance sampling representation is

$$
\begin{aligned}
p\left(H_{0}\right) & =E_{\hat{p}}\left\{\frac{p\left(H_{0} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{\hat{p}\left(H_{-1} \mid H_{0}\right)} \cdots \frac{p\left(H_{-m+1} \mid H_{-m}\right)}{\hat{p}\left(H_{-m} \mid H_{-m+1}\right)} p\left(H_{-m}\right)\right\} \\
& =E_{\hat{p}}\left\{\frac{p\left(\mathcal{H}_{\rightarrow}\right)}{\hat{p}\left(\mathcal{H}_{\leftarrow}\right)}\right\} .
\end{aligned}
$$
comments.message: Statement (35) initially gave me the impression that \(p(H_j\mid H'_{j-1})\) was being used both for the recursion coefficients in equation (34) and for the forward transition probabilities \(p_\theta(H_i\mid H_{i-1})\) of the history-generating process defined earlier. Since these objects are distinct, this overloading of the symbol \(p(\cdot\mid\cdot)\) could momentarily mislead a reader trying to relate (35) directly to the coalescent dynamics. It might help if the discussion explicitly stated that in (35) the terms \(p(H_i\mid H_{i-1})\) are still the coefficients from the linear system (34), and that \(\hat p(\cdot\mid\cdot)\) is a probability kernel constructed from \(\hat\pi\) used purely as a proposal for solving those equations, rather than as an approximation to the forward transition kernel in equation (1).

comments.title: Incorrect recursive weight formula in SIS discussion
comments.paragraph:
As with many SIS applications, both Stephens and Donnelly, and Griffiths and Tavaré (1994) propose trial densities $q_{0}(\mathcal{H})$ of the form

$$
q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})=\prod_{i=0}^{-(m-1)} q_{\theta}\left(H_{i-1} \mid H_{i}\right)
$$

For such constructions we define the current weight (for $t \leqslant m$ )

$$
w_{-t}=\frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t}\right) \ldots p_{\theta}\left(H_{0} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right) \ldots q_{0}\left(H_{-1} \mid H_{0}\right)} \equiv w_{-(t-1)} \frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right)}
$$
comments.quote:
As with many SIS applications, both Stephens and Donnelly, and Griffiths and Tavaré (1994) propose trial densities $q_{0}(\mathcal{H})$ of the form

$$
q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})=\prod_{i=0}^{-(m-1)} q_{\theta}\left(H_{i-1} \mid H_{i}\right)
$$

For such constructions we define the current weight (for $t \leqslant m$ )

$$
w_{-t}=\frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t}\right) \ldots p_{\theta}\left(H_{0} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right) \ldots q_{0}\left(H_{-1} \mid H_{0}\right)} \equiv w_{-(t-1)} \frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right)}
$$
comments.message:
The recursive definition of the current weight $w_{-t}$ in the Chen–Liu discussion appears to contain a small indexing typo. From the explicit product expression,
\[
w_{-t}=\frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t}\right)\cdots p_{\theta}\left(H_{0} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right)\cdots q_{0}\left(H_{-1} \mid H_{0}\right)},
\]
one obtains the recursion
\[
w_{-t}=w_{-(t-1)}\,\frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right)}.
\]
The printed version instead has $p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1}\right)$ in this last factor, which is inconsistent with both the preceding line and the Markov structure, and should be corrected to condition on $H_{-t}$. In addition, the product notation $\prod_{i=0}^{-(m-1)} q_{\theta}(H_{i-1}\mid H_i)$ is somewhat non-standard; since $i$ is running over $0,-1,\dots,-(m-1)$ this is still correct, but a brief clarification or reindexing would improve readability.

zero_shot.label: Zero Shot
zero_shot.overall_feedback: This is a seminal paper introducing an improved importance sampling method for likelihood inference in population genetics. The paper is generally well-written with clear exposition of the theoretical framework. The authors provide a rigorous characterization of the optimal proposal distribution and develop a practical approximation. The numerical comparisons are comprehensive. However, there are a few technical issues that warrant attention: a potential sign error or inconsistency in the constant of proportionality formula, some ambiguity in the definition of the history notation, and a potential issue with the transition probability expressions that require careful verification.

comments.title: Inconsistency in constant C formula between theorem statement and equation (1)
comments.quote:
The constant of proportionality $C$ is given by
$$
C=\frac{n(n-1+\theta)}{2},
$$
where $n$ is the number of chromosomes in $H_{i}$.
comments.explanation: I was initially puzzled by the relationship between this constant C and the rate parameters. Looking at equation (1), the forward transition probabilities have denominators involving (n-1+θ) where n is the number of chromosomes in H_{i-1}, not H_i. In the backward direction from H_i to H_{i-1}, if H_i has n chromosomes, then H_{i-1} has either n-1 chromosomes (if the forward transition was a split) or n chromosomes (if it was a mutation). The theorem states C = n(n-1+θ)/2 where n is in H_i. However, to properly normalize backward transition probabilities, one typically needs to account for the total rate at which events happen going forward from states that could lead to H_i. The total rate when there are k lineages is λ_k = k(k-1+θ)/2 (from Algorithm 1). The claim that C = n(n-1+θ)/2 seems to assume all transitions backward from H_i (which has n chromosomes) should sum to 1, but the dimensional analysis is unclear. The proof references Stephens (2000) for this value but does not show the derivation. While this may be correct, the justification appears insufficient for such a key result.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for history sequence ordering
comments.quote: The history $\mathcal{H}$ thus includes a record of the states ( $H_{-m}, H_{-(m-1)}, \ldots, H_{1}, H_{0}$ ) visited by a Markov process beginning with the genetic type $H_{-m} \in E$ of the MRCA and ending with the genetic types $H_{0} \in E^{n}$ corresponding to the genetic types of a random sample.
comments.explanation: The notation uses negative indices with H_{-m} being the MRCA (a single type in E) and H_0 being the sample (n types in E^n). However, the sequence is written as H_{-m}, H_{-(m-1)}, ..., H_1, H_0 which would include H_1 as a separate state between H_0 and the earlier states. Looking at the example in Figure 1, the history is given as 9 configurations going from {C} to {C,T,T,T,T}. If m represents the number of events, then there should be m+1 states. The sequence H_{-m},...,H_1,H_0 suggests states indexed from -m to 0, which is m+1 states, but the inclusion of H_1 in the sequence notation is confusing. It appears H_1 should not be in this sequence - the intended notation is likely H_{-m}, H_{-(m-1)}, ..., H_{-1}, H_0 (from index -m to 0). This ambiguity could mislead readers about the indexing convention.

comments.title: Missing specification of what happens when n_α = 0 in transition probabilities
comments.quote: $q_{\theta}^{*}\left(H_{i-1} \mid H_{i}\right)= \begin{cases}C^{-1} \frac{\theta}{2} n_{\alpha} \frac{\pi\left(\beta \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}{\pi\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)} P_{\beta \alpha} & \text { if } H_{i-1}=H_{i}-\alpha+\beta \\ C^{-1}\binom{n_{\alpha}}{2} \frac{1}{\pi\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)} & \text { if } H_{i-1}=H_{i}-\alpha, \\ 0 & \text { otherwise },\end{cases}$
comments.explanation: The formula involves n_α, the number of chromosomes of type α in H_i. For the coalescence case (H_{i-1} = H_i - α), the formula requires n_α ≥ 2 for the binomial coefficient to be non-zero. For the mutation case, n_α ≥ 1 is required. These constraints are implicit from the context (you can only coalesce types that exist and have at least 2 copies), but they are not explicitly stated. More importantly, the notation H_i - α requires that α is present in H_i. A careful reader might wonder about the behavior when trying to remove a type that doesn't exist. While the 'otherwise' case catches this, making these conditions explicit would improve clarity for implementation.

comments.title: Potential issue with matrix inverse existence in equation (19)
comments.quote:
$M^{(n)}=\left(1-\lambda_{n}\right)\left(I-\lambda_{n} P\right)^{-1}$
where $\lambda_{n}=\theta /(n+\theta)$.
comments.explanation: The formula requires the matrix (I - λ_n P) to be invertible. Since P is a stochastic matrix (rows sum to 1), its eigenvalues have magnitude at most 1, with 1 being an eigenvalue. For λ_n = θ/(n+θ) < 1 (assuming θ > 0 and n ≥ 1), the matrix (I - λ_n P) has eigenvalues 1 - λ_n μ where μ are eigenvalues of P. If P has eigenvalue 1 (which it does, being stochastic), then (I - λ_n P) has eigenvalue 1 - λ_n > 0, so this doesn't cause singularity. However, if P were to have eigenvalue 1/λ_n (which would require an eigenvalue > 1, impossible for stochastic matrices), there would be an issue. The authors should perhaps note that the inverse exists because λ_n < 1 and P's eigenvalues have magnitude ≤ 1, making all eigenvalues of (I - λ_n P) strictly positive. This is a minor point since the mathematics is correct, but the existence of the inverse is non-trivially assumed.

comments.title: Inconsistency between equation (2) and surrounding text regarding sample ordering
comments.quote: $\pi_{\theta}\left(A_{n} \mid \mathcal{H}\right)=\pi_{\theta}\left(A_{n} \mid H_{0}\right)= \begin{cases}\left(\prod_{\alpha \in E} n_{\alpha}!\right) / n! & \text { if } H_{0} \text { is consistent with } A_{n} \\ 0 & \text { otherwise. }\end{cases}$
comments.explanation: The text states 'To give an ordered sample, a random order must be assigned to the n types in H_0', and A_n is defined as an ordered sample A_n = (a_1, a_2, ..., a_n). The formula (∏_{α∈E} n_α!)/n! gives the probability of obtaining a specific ordered sample A_n from an unordered configuration H_0. This is the correct multinomial probability for this situation. However, I initially found the notation confusing because π_θ(A_n | H) appears to condition on the entire history H, but then the formula only depends on H_0. The equality π_θ(A_n | H) = π_θ(A_n | H_0) is stated but deserves brief justification - it follows because the ordering is assigned independently of the history leading to H_0, given H_0 itself. This could be made more explicit for clarity.

local.label: Local
local.overall_feedback: This paper by Stephens and Donnelly presents a novel importance sampling algorithm for likelihood-based inference in population genetics, addressing a computationally challenging problem of significant practical importance. The writing is exceptionally clear and well-organized, with the authors providing excellent contextualization of their contribution within both the population genetics and computational statistics literatures. The abstract and introduction effectively communicate the key innovation—approximating the optimal proposal distribution through detailed analysis of genealogical processes—and honestly assess the method's performance relative to existing IS and MCMC approaches rather than overclaiming superiority. The framing as a missing data problem with connections to broader inference challenges in high-dimensional stochastic processes appropriately positions the work for a statistical audience. However, based on the excerpt provided, the paper would benefit from earlier inclusion of a concrete illustrative example or intuitive explanation of the genealogical structure being exploited, as readers unfamiliar with coalescent theory may struggle to appreciate the technical innovations until much later in the paper. Additionally, while the claim of "several orders of magnitude" improvement is compelling, the introduction could briefly indicate the absolute computational costs involved to help readers gauge practical applicability.

comments.title: Underspecified condition for Fleming-Viot domain of attraction
comments.quote: Our methods apply to a wide range of specific demographic models (formally, those which are in the domain of attraction of the Fleming-Viot diffusion; Donnelly and Kurtz (1999)).
comments.explanation: Initially I wondered whether this parenthetical provides sufficient information for readers to understand which models qualify. The Fleming-Viot domain of attraction is a technical condition that depends on specific moment conditions and exchangeability properties of the offspring distribution. While this is an introductory section and a citation is provided, the passage then gives the Wright-Fisher model as the only concrete example. A reader unfamiliar with Donnelly and Kurtz (1999) would have difficulty determining whether other common models (e.g., Moran model, models with selection, variable population size) fall within this class. However, given that para 13 states 'The methods discussed here have been extended to several more general models' and this is clearly scene-setting material, the forward reference to the cited work is arguably sufficient for an overview section. This is borderline but the lack of even informal characterization of what 'domain of attraction' entails could mislead readers about the generality of the approach.

comments.title: Mutation rate formula may have inconsistent scaling
comments.quote: $\theta / 2=N \mu / \nu^{2}$
comments.explanation: Initially, I was concerned about the factor of 2 in this formula. The standard coalescent parameterization defines θ = 2Nμ for diploid populations or θ = Nμ for haploid populations (with appropriate scaling). Here, the authors state θ/2 = Nμ/ν², which implies θ = 2Nμ/ν². Checking para 17, the timescale is N/ν² generations. For a Wright-Fisher model with ν² = 1, this would give θ = 2Nμ, consistent with the standard diploid convention. However, para 14 explicitly states they are considering haploid demographic models. In haploid Wright-Fisher, the coalescent rate is 1/N per pair per generation (not 1/2N), so the mutation rate should scale as θ = Nμ, not 2Nμ. The factor of 2 appears inconsistent with the haploid framing established earlier, though this could be a deliberate notational choice to maintain consistency with standard coalescent literature that often uses the diploid convention regardless of ploidy. This warrants clarification.

comments.title: Unstated assumption about reversibility of mutation chain
comments.quote: the distribution of the type of the MRCA is given by the stationary distribution of the (mutation) Markov chain $P$
comments.explanation: The claim that the MRCA type follows the stationary distribution of P at stationarity is correct under neutrality, but this relies on the population having reached equilibrium with respect to the mutation process. Para 16 states that P has a unique stationary distribution, which is necessary but the claim also implicitly requires that the mutation process has been running long enough for the population type frequencies to equilibrate to this distribution. While 'at stationarity' in para 17 addresses this, a careful reader might wonder whether additional conditions on P (such as irreducibility, which would follow from having a unique stationary distribution) are needed. Checking the context, para 16 does assume a unique stationary distribution exists, which together with the stationarity assumption adequately justifies this claim. On reflection, this is sufficiently justified for an overview passage.

comments.title: Transition probabilities may not sum to one
comments.quote: p_{\theta}\left(H_{i} \mid H_{i-1}\right)= \begin{cases}\frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} \frac{\theta}{n-1+\theta} P_{\alpha \beta} & \text { if } H_{i}=H_{i-1}-\alpha+\beta \\ \frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} \frac{n-1}{n-1+\theta} & \text { if } H_{i}=H_{i-1}+\alpha \\ 0 & \text { otherwise }\end{cases}
comments.explanation: I checked whether these transition probabilities sum to 1 over all possible next states H_i. Summing over all mutation events (α→β for all α in H_{i-1} and all β): Σ_α Σ_β (n_α/n)(θ/(n-1+θ))P_{αβ} = (θ/(n-1+θ)) Σ_α (n_α/n) Σ_β P_{αβ} = θ/(n-1+θ), since Σ_β P_{αβ}=1 and Σ_α n_α = n. Summing over all split events: Σ_α (n_α/n)(n-1)/(n-1+θ) = (n-1)/(n-1+θ). Total: θ/(n-1+θ) + (n-1)/(n-1+θ) = (n-1+θ)/(n-1+θ) = 1. So the probabilities do sum to 1. However, I initially worried about whether mutations α→α (when P_{αα}>0) are handled correctly—the formula gives nonzero probability to H_i = H_{i-1}-α+α = H_{i-1}, meaning a 'mutation' that doesn't change the state. The authors acknowledge this subtlety in para 24, noting that H contains transition details 'which may not be uniquely determined by the list of states if P_{αα}>0'. This means H is actually more than just the sequence of states—it includes event labels. The formula is correct for the distribution over events, but this could mislead readers who interpret H purely as a state sequence. The notation H_i = H_{i-1}-α+α is potentially confusing since it suggests no change occurred.

comments.title: Derivation from Algorithm 1 to transition probabilities is non-trivial and unjustified
comments.quote: It follows from algorithm 1 that the distribution $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ is defined by the distribution $\psi(\cdot)$ of the type of the MRCA, the stopping procedure (step 3 of the algorithm) and the Markov transition probabilities
comments.explanation: The authors state the transition probabilities 'follow from' Algorithm 1, but the derivation involves a subtle step. In Algorithm 1 Step 2, with k lines, one line is chosen uniformly at random, then either split (prob (k-1)/(k-1+θ)) or mutated (prob θ/(k-1+θ)). The history H removes the identity of which line is involved. To get p_θ(H_i|H_{i-1}), we need: P(choose type α) × P(event type). Choosing a line of type α has probability n_α/k = n_α/n (where n = k is the current count). For mutation to β: (n_α/n)(θ/(n-1+θ))P_{αβ}. For split of type α: (n_α/n)((n-1)/(n-1+θ)). This matches the formula. However, the key insight that collapsing over line identities preserves the Markov property for H is non-trivial—it relies on exchangeability properties of the coalescent. The claim that H forms a Markov chain with these specific transition probabilities deserves more justification, though experts in the field may consider this standard.

comments.title: Extraordinarily wide range for required sample size
comments.quote: reliable estimation will require values of $M$ (in the range $10^{6}-10^{100}$ for the examples that we consider here)
comments.explanation: The claimed range of $10^6$ to $10^{100}$ spans 94 orders of magnitude, which is an extraordinarily wide range that raises questions about its informativeness. While I understand the authors are making a rhetorical point about computational impracticability, $10^{100}$ (a googol) is far beyond any conceivable computation—the number of atoms in the observable universe is roughly $10^{80}$. The lower bound $10^6$ is actually computationally feasible with modern hardware, so it seems odd to lump these together. A careful reader might wonder: what determines where in this vast range a particular problem falls? Is this range derived from any principled calculation (e.g., based on the probability that a random genealogy is consistent with observed data), or is it merely illustrative hyperbole? The authors don't provide justification for these specific bounds or reference where 'the examples we consider here' are analyzed to yield such estimates. This could be seen as an insufficiently justified claim, though it may be elaborated elsewhere in the paper.

comments.title: Function F is undefined and its dependence on θ is not specified
comments.quote: E\left\{\prod_{j=0}^{\tau} F\left(B_{j}\right) \mid B_{0}=A_{n}\right\}
comments.explanation: The passage introduces a function F(B_j) that appears in the product within the expectation, but F is never defined in the provided context. More critically, since L(θ) on the left-hand side depends on θ, and the Markov chain {B_j} is described without explicit θ-dependence, the function F should presumably depend on θ (i.e., F_θ or F(B_j; θ)) for the equation to be consistent. The text in para 41 mentions that 'naïve application requires simulation from a different Markov chain for each value of θ,' suggesting the θ-dependence enters somewhere—likely through F or the chain's transition kernel—but this is left implicit. While para 38 mentions 'recursions for probabilities of interest' from Griffiths and Tavaré (1994a), a reader cannot verify the correctness of equation (5) or understand the method without knowing what F represents.

comments.title: Transition dynamics of the Markov chain B_j are underspecified
comments.quote: where $B_{0}, B_{1}, \ldots, B_{\tau}$ is a particular set-valued Markov chain stopped the first time $\tau$ that it has cardinality 1
comments.explanation: The passage describes the Markov chain as 'set-valued' and stopped when it reaches cardinality 1, but provides no information about its transition kernel or how it relates to the coalescent/genealogical structure described earlier. For the likelihood representation to be meaningful, readers need to understand how states B_j evolve—are they sets of haplotypes that coalesce? What are the transition probabilities? The phrase 'a particular set-valued Markov chain' acknowledges specificity but doesn't deliver it. While forward reference to Griffiths and Tavaré (1994a) is given, this is a key methodological equation and some indication of the chain's construction would help readers assess whether the Monte Carlo scheme is correctly described.

comments.title: Missing denominator term in first line of derivation
comments.quote: \frac{L(\theta)}{L\left(\theta_{0}\right)} =\frac{\int P_{\theta}\left(\mathcal{T}, A_{n}\right) \mathrm{d} \mathcal{T}}{P_{\theta_{0}}\left(A_{n}\right)}
comments.explanation: Initially, I expected the ratio of likelihoods to be expressed symmetrically. The numerator correctly shows L(θ) = ∫P_θ(T, A_n)dT (marginalizing over the missing data). However, the denominator shows P_{θ_0}(A_n) rather than ∫P_{θ_0}(T, A_n)dT. While these are equal by the same marginalization identity, this creates an apparent asymmetry. Upon reflection, this is actually correct and intentional: the authors write P_{θ_0}(A_n) = L(θ_0) directly without showing the integral, which is valid. However, a careful reader might momentarily wonder why the integral form appears only in the numerator. This is not strictly an error but could be made clearer by noting L(θ_0) = P_{θ_0}(A_n) = ∫P_{θ_0}(T, A_n)dT. Given that this is a standard importance sampling derivation and the result is correct, I consider this a very minor issue of presentation clarity rather than a true error.

comments.title: Implicit assumption that importance sampling ratio is well-behaved
comments.quote: \approx \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \frac{P_{\theta}\left(A_{n}, \mathcal{T}^{(i)}\right)}{P_{\theta_{0}}\left(A_{n}, \mathcal{T}^{(i)}\right)}
comments.explanation: The transition from the integral to the Monte Carlo sum is standard importance sampling, which is mathematically justified. However, this approximation implicitly assumes that P_{θ_0}(T, A_n) > 0 whenever P_θ(T, A_n) > 0 (i.e., the support of the proposal distribution covers that of the target). More practically, the approximation's quality depends heavily on θ being 'close' to θ_0 — when θ differs substantially from θ_0, the variance of the importance weights can become very large, making the estimator unreliable. The text in para 42-44 mentions this is 'a relative likelihood surface' and cites Geyer and Thompson (1992), which does address these issues. For a methods paper, one might expect explicit acknowledgment that this estimator can have high variance for θ far from θ_0, but given the citation to the original importance sampling literature, this is perhaps adequately covered by reference. This is a borderline concern about underspecification of method limitations.

comments.title: Missing summation over α in the transition from line 2 to line 3
comments.quote: =\sum_{\gamma \in E} \sum_{\xi \in E}\left\{\frac{n_{\gamma}}{n} \frac{n_{\xi}}{n+1} M_{\xi \beta}^{(n+1)}+\frac{n_{\gamma}}{n(n+1)} M_{\gamma \xi}^{(n)} M_{\xi \beta}^{(n+1)}\right\}
comments.explanation: In line 2, we have a sum over α, γ, and ξ. The term (n_ξ + δ_{αξ})/(n+1) splits into two parts. For the first part (n_ξ/(n+1)), summing over α of M^{(n)}_{γα} should yield a factor since M^{(n)} is a stochastic matrix (rows sum to 1), giving Σ_α M^{(n)}_{γα} = 1. This is implicitly used but not stated. For the second part involving δ_{αξ}, the sum over α collapses ξ to α, yielding M^{(n)}_{γξ} M^{(n+1)}_{ξβ}. The derivation appears correct but the step is non-trivial: it relies on the unstated assumption that M^{(n)} is row-stochastic (each row sums to 1). While this is likely true given the probabilistic interpretation, it represents a skipped justification that could confuse readers trying to verify the algebra.

comments.title: Unexplained appearance of M^{(n+1)} factor in the first term of line 3
comments.quote: \frac{n_{\gamma}}{n} \frac{n_{\xi}}{n+1} M_{\xi \beta}^{(n+1)}
comments.explanation: Looking at line 2, after summing M^{(n)}_{γα} over α (assuming row-stochasticity gives 1), we get (n_γ/n)(n_ξ/(n+1))M^{(n+1)}_{ξβ}. However, in line 4, this contributes to 'n M^{(n+1)}' inside the bracket. The sum Σ_ξ (n_ξ/n) M^{(n+1)}_{ξβ} = [η M^{(n+1)}]_β, and Σ_γ (n_γ/n) = 1. So we get (n/(n+1))[η M^{(n+1)}]_β, which matches the final form. The algebra checks out, but the derivation skips showing why Σ_γ n_γ/n = 1 and how the products of sums separate. These steps, while straightforward, represent condensed reasoning that could benefit from brief explanation.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for the identity derived from equation (22)
comments.quote:
From equation (22) we have

$$
1=\sum_{\beta \in E} \frac{\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}\left(\frac{\theta}{n-1+\theta} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{n_{\alpha}-1}{n-1+\theta}\right)
$$
comments.explanation: The passage claims this identity follows 'from equation (22)' but equation (22) is not shown in the provided context. While it's acceptable to reference external equations, the derivation here is non-trivial and central to the proof. The expression involves summing over all types β of a ratio of conditional probabilities times a weighted combination of mutation and coalescence terms. A careful reader would need to verify: (1) that equation (22) indeed provides a normalization condition for these conditional probabilities, and (2) that the specific form with the factor (n_α - 1) in the coalescence term (rather than, say, n_α or some other quantity) is correct. The second term having (n_α - 1) rather than n_α in the numerator is crucial for the subsequent algebra to work out, but without seeing equation (22), one cannot verify this is the correct coefficient. This is a key step in the proof that warrants more explicit justification.

comments.title: Unjustified algebraic substitution in second equality
comments.quote: =\sum_{\beta \in E} \frac{\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)} C^{-1} \frac{\theta}{2} n_{\alpha} P_{\beta \alpha}+\frac{1}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)} C^{-1}\binom{n_{\alpha}}{2}
comments.explanation: The passage moves from the first line to the second line by implicitly using that $\frac{\theta}{n-1+\theta} \frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} = C^{-1} \frac{\theta}{2}$ and $\frac{n_\alpha}{n} \frac{n_{\alpha}-1}{n-1+\theta} = C^{-1}\binom{n_\alpha}{2}$. For the second substitution: $\frac{n_\alpha}{n} \frac{n_{\alpha}-1}{n-1+\theta} = \frac{n_\alpha(n_\alpha-1)}{n(n-1+\theta)}$, while $C^{-1}\binom{n_\alpha}{2} = C^{-1}\frac{n_\alpha(n_\alpha-1)}{2}$. These are equal only if $C = \frac{n(n-1+\theta)}{2}$. For the first substitution: $\frac{\theta}{n-1+\theta} \frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} = C^{-1} \frac{\theta}{2}$ requires $C = \frac{n(n-1+\theta)}{2n_\alpha}$. But this would make $C$ depend on $n_\alpha$, whereas the normalizing constant $C$ should be independent of which type $\alpha$ we're considering. The derivation appears to require different values of $C$ for the two terms unless $n_\alpha = 1$ always, which contradicts the coalescence term requiring $n_\alpha \geq 2$. The value of $C$ is not explicitly given in the provided context (equation 22 is referenced but not shown), so while the authors may have a consistent definition elsewhere, the algebraic step as presented involves a substitution that is non-trivial and potentially inconsistent.

comments.title: Coalescence term loses summation over β without justification
comments.quote: +\frac{1}{\hat{\pi}\left(\alpha \mid H_{i}-\alpha\right)} C^{-1}\binom{n_{\alpha}}{2}
comments.explanation: In the first line, the coalescence-related term $\frac{n_\alpha}{n} \frac{n_{\alpha}-1}{n-1+\theta}$ appears inside the summation $\sum_{\beta \in E}$. In the second line, the corresponding term $\frac{1}{\hat{\pi}(\alpha|H_i-\alpha)} C^{-1}\binom{n_\alpha}{2}$ appears outside any summation. This transformation is valid only if $\sum_{\beta \in E} \frac{\hat{\pi}(\beta|H_i-\alpha)}{\hat{\pi}(\alpha|H_i-\alpha)} = \frac{1}{\hat{\pi}(\alpha|H_i-\alpha)}$, which in turn requires $\sum_{\beta \in E} \hat{\pi}(\beta|H_i-\alpha) = 1$. While this is likely true (probabilities summing to 1), this step is not explicitly justified in the passage. A careful reader might be confused about how the sum disappeared, and this warrants a brief note given that it's a key step in the proof.

comments.title: Potentially missing or unexplained term in importance sampling estimator
comments.quote: P_{\theta}\left(A_{n}, O\right) \approx \frac{1}{M} \sum_{i=1}^{M} \pi_{\theta}\left(A_{n} \mid \mathcal{H}^{(i)}\right) P_{\theta}\left(O \mid \mathcal{H}^{(i)}\right) \frac{P_{\theta}\left(\mathcal{H}^{(i)}\right)}{Q_{\theta}\left(\mathcal{H}^{(i)}\right)}
comments.explanation: The standard importance sampling identity for estimating P(A_n, O) would be: draw H from Q, then compute the expectation of P(A_n, O, H)/Q(H). This should give P(A_n, O | H) · P(H) / Q(H). The formula presented has π_θ(A_n | H) · P_θ(O | H) · P_θ(H) / Q_θ(H). For this to correctly estimate P_θ(A_n, O), we need π_θ(A_n | H) · P_θ(O | H) · P_θ(H) = P_θ(A_n, O, H). This requires π_θ(A_n | H) · P_θ(O | H) = P_θ(A_n, O | H). If O is conditionally independent of A_n given H, then P_θ(A_n, O | H) = P_θ(A_n | H) · P_θ(O | H), which would require π_θ(A_n | H) = P_θ(A_n | H). However, the notation π_θ is introduced separately (in equation 9, referenced in para 154), and without seeing that definition, it's unclear whether π_θ(A_n | H) equals P_θ(A_n | H) or represents something else (like a probability on the observed types). The authors should clarify whether π_θ(A_n | H) = P_θ(A_n | H) or explain the relationship, especially since π notation often denotes something other than a standard probability measure.

comments.title: Optimal proposal distribution formula may be missing a normalization factor or likelihood term
comments.quote: $Q_{\theta}^{*}(\mathcal{H}) \propto P_{\theta}\left(\mathcal{H} \mid A_{n}\right) P\{O \mid r(\mathcal{H})\}$
comments.explanation: Initially, I considered whether this formula for the optimal proposal distribution is correctly derived. The standard result for optimal importance sampling is that Q* should be proportional to the integrand of the importance sampling estimator. Looking at equation (159), the estimand involves π_θ(A_n|H)P_θ(O|H)P_θ(H)/Q_θ(H). For the optimal proposal minimizing variance, we would typically want Q*(H) ∝ |f(H)|P_θ(H) where f(H) is the function being estimated. The authors write Q* ∝ P_θ(H|A_n)P{O|r(H)}, but P_θ(H|A_n) ∝ π_θ(A_n|H)P_θ(H). So this gives Q* ∝ π_θ(A_n|H)P_θ(H)P{O|r(H)}. However, the standard optimal IS distribution for estimating E[f(X)] is Q*(x) ∝ f(x)p(x), and here we're estimating P_θ(A_n,O), so f(H) = π_θ(A_n|H)P_θ(O|H). The optimal would be Q* ∝ π_θ(A_n|H)P_θ(O|H)P_θ(H) = P_θ(H|A_n)P_θ(A_n)P_θ(O|H). The authors' formula uses P{O|r(H)} without the θ subscript (consistent with their assumption that this is independent of θ), but the formula appears correct given the proportionality. The concern is resolved by context - the authors are using proportionality and the formula correctly captures that the optimal proposal should weight histories by both their posterior probability given the genetic data and by the outgroup likelihood.

comments.title: Missing integration bounds in equation (31)
comments.quote: \int \exp (-t) F_{\alpha_{1} \beta_{1}}^{(\theta, t, n)} \ldots F_{\alpha_{l} \beta_{l}}^{(\theta, t, n)} \mathrm{d} t
comments.explanation: The integral is written without explicit bounds. From the context in para 185, the time t is drawn from an exponential distribution with rate parameter 1, which means t ranges from 0 to infinity. The factor exp(-t) in the integrand corresponds to this exponential density. While a reader familiar with the derivation can infer the bounds should be from 0 to ∞, the notation as written is technically incomplete. This is a minor notational issue, but since para 188 mentions approximating this integral using Gaussian quadrature, explicitly stating the bounds would help clarify the computational procedure. A careful reader implementing this formula might need to verify the integration domain.

comments.title: Missing integral sign in Gaussian quadrature approximation
comments.explanation: Looking at equation (31) in para 186, the exact formula has an integral over t with exp(-t) as part of the integrand. Para 188 states this integral 'may be approximated by using Gaussian quadrature.' In standard Gaussian quadrature for integrals of the form ∫₀^∞ exp(-t)g(t)dt (Gauss-Laguerre quadrature), the exp(-t) weight function is absorbed into the quadrature weights wᵢ, so it should not appear explicitly. However, I initially wondered whether the approximation sign (≈) should be used instead of equality (=), since this is explicitly described as an approximation. The text in para 188 uses the word 'approximated' but the equation uses '='. This is a minor notational inconsistency—the formula is substantively correct for Gaussian quadrature, but presenting an approximation with an equality sign without any indication (like ≈) could be slightly misleading, though para 190 does clarify it's an approximation.

comments.title: Quadrature type and weight specification underspecified
comments.quote: where $t_{1}, \ldots, t_{s}$ are the quadrature points, and $w_{1}, \ldots, w_{s}$ are the corresponding quadrature weights
comments.explanation: The integral in equation (31) is ∫₀^∞ exp(-t)[...]dt, which has the form suited for Gauss-Laguerre quadrature. Para 190 mentions they used s=4 quadrature points but does not specify which type of Gaussian quadrature was employed (Gauss-Laguerre, Gauss-Hermite, or a transformed Gauss-Legendre). Different quadrature rules have different nodes and weights, and for reproducibility a reader would need to know which rule was used. The reference to Evans (1993) provides a general source but not the specific choice. While this may seem like a minor detail, for implementation purposes knowing whether standard Gauss-Laguerre nodes/weights were used (which would be natural given the exp(-t) factor) versus some other approach matters for exact reproduction of results.

comments.title: Implicit definition of likelihood function L(θ) without explicit statement
comments.quote: \frac{\int p\left(A_{n} \mid \theta, \mathcal{H}\right) p(\mathcal{H} \mid \theta) \mathrm{d} \mathcal{H}}{\iint p\left(A_{n} \mid \theta, \mathcal{H}\right) p(\mathcal{H} \mid \theta) \mathrm{d} \mathcal{H} p(\theta) \mathrm{d} \theta} p(\theta) \\ & =\frac{L(\theta) p(\theta)}{\int L(\theta) p(\theta) \mathrm{d} \theta}
comments.explanation: The derivation implicitly defines L(θ) = ∫p(Aₙ|θ,H)p(H|θ)dH, which is the marginal likelihood of the data given θ (integrating out the nuisance variable H). While this is a standard definition in Bayesian contexts, the author introduces L(θ) without explicitly stating this definition. A careful reader can infer it from the structure of the equation, but given that the subsequent discussion in para 208-209 hinges on understanding what L(θ) represents and how to approximate it, an explicit statement would prevent ambiguity. This is a minor concern since the implicit definition is recoverable, but in a discussion paper where clarity is paramount, this could mislead readers unfamiliar with this marginalization approach to likelihood.

comments.title: Constant c defined ambiguously as both normalizing constant and its reciprocal
comments.quote: =\frac{L(\theta) p(\theta)}{\int L(\theta) p(\theta) \mathrm{d} \theta}=c L(\theta) p(\theta)
comments.explanation: The final step writes p(θ|Aₙ) = cL(θ)p(θ), which implies c = 1/∫L(θ)p(θ)dθ. However, the notation 'c' for a normalizing constant is potentially confusing because in para 208 the author states 'obtaining L(θ) or c from p(θ,H|Aₙ) is much more challenging.' This suggests c is meant to be the evidence p(Aₙ) = ∫L(θ)p(θ)dθ, but as written in the equation, c is actually its reciprocal (1/p(Aₙ)). The inconsistency is whether c represents the evidence or the inverse normalizing constant. While experienced readers will recognize this as a standard normalization issue, the ambiguity could confuse readers trying to connect the mathematical derivation to the subsequent discussion about computing c.

comments.title: Factorial notation error in denominator
comments.explanation: The denominator shows $m^{i!}$, which would be parsed as $m$ raised to the power $i!$ (i factorial). This is almost certainly a typo for $m^i!$ (meaning $(m^i)!$, i.e., the factorial of the number of mutations $m^i$). The expression is meant to be the conditional posterior density of θ given a genealogy, which under the standard Poisson mutation model should have the form of a Gamma distribution. The correct Poisson probability mass function has $m!$ (factorial of the count) in the denominator, so the intended expression should be $m^{i}!$ or better written as $(m^i)!$. The placement of the superscript $i$ inside the factorial notation creates an incorrect mathematical expression.

comments.title: Missing normalization constant for proper density
comments.quote: \pi\left(\theta \mid \mathcal{H}^{i}, A_{n}\right)=\frac{T^{i}}{2} \frac{\left(T^{i} \theta / 2\right)^{m^{i}} \exp \left(-T^{i} \theta / 2\right)}{m^{i!}}
comments.explanation: Looking at this expression as a density in θ, the structure resembles a Gamma distribution. For a Gamma(α, β) density, the form is (β^α / Γ(α)) θ^{α-1} exp(-βθ). Here we have terms proportional to θ^{m^i} exp(-T^i θ/2), suggesting a Gamma(m^i + 1, T^i/2) distribution. However, the leading factor T^i/2 and the factorial in the denominator don't combine to give the correct Gamma normalization constant, which should be (T^i/2)^{m^i+1} / Γ(m^i+1) = (T^i/2)^{m^i+1} / m^i!. The given expression has (T^i/2) × (T^i/2)^{m^i} / m^i! = (T^i/2)^{m^i+1} / m^i!, which actually does work out correctly if we interpret m^{i!} as m^i!. So this concern is resolved once the notation issue is fixed, but the unusual presentation makes verification difficult.

comments.title: Index variable 'n' conflicts with established notation for sample size
comments.quote: \hat{\pi}\left(\theta \mid A_{n}\right)=\frac{1}{n} \sum^{n} \pi\left(\theta \mid \mathcal{H}^{i}, A_{n}\right)
comments.explanation: The notation $A_n$ throughout the paper (and in standard coalescent literature) uses $n$ to denote the sample size of alleles/sequences. However, in this equation, $n$ is reused as the number of MCMC samples being averaged over. This creates a notational collision: the same symbol appears to mean two different things within the same equation. Para 265 clarifies that 5 simulations of 10^7 iterations were run with sampling every 500 iterations, so the number of samples per chain would be 20,000—clearly distinct from any genetic sample size $n$. A different index variable (e.g., $N$ or $M$ for the number of posterior samples) would avoid this ambiguity. A careful reader might momentarily misinterpret this as somehow involving the genetic sample size.

comments.title: Summation index not specified in notation
comments.quote: \sum^{n} \pi\left(\theta \mid \mathcal{H}^{i}, A_{n}\right)
comments.explanation: The summation is written as $\sum^{n}$ without specifying the index variable or its starting value. While it is clear from context that the sum should be $\sum_{i=1}^{n}$ (summing over sampled genealogies $\mathcal{H}^i$), the notation as written is incomplete. This is a minor issue but could cause confusion, especially given the already-noted ambiguity about what $n$ represents. Standard mathematical convention requires specifying both the index and its range.

comments.title: Product index bounds appear inverted for forward-time notation
comments.quote: $q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})=\prod_{i=0}^{-(m-1)} q_{\theta}\left(H_{i-1} \mid H_{i}\right)$
comments.explanation: Initially I was confused by the product running from i=0 down to -(m-1), which is unconventional notation. Looking at the context, this is a coalescent model where time runs backwards (negative indices represent going back in time). The product should decompose the joint density of the history. However, there's an issue: when i=0, we get q(H_{-1}|H_0), and when i=-(m-1), we get q(H_{-m}|H_{-(m-1)}). This seems internally consistent for a backward-time coalescent. But comparing with para 285, the denominator shows terms like q_0(H_{-t}|H_{-(t-1)})...q_0(H_{-1}|H_0), which conditions on the *more ancient* state (more negative index). This is reversed from the product formula which conditions on the less negative index. Either the product formula should use q(H_i|H_{i-1}) or the indices in para 285 should be swapped. The conditioning direction appears inconsistent between the two equations.

comments.title: Subscript inconsistency: q_θ vs q_0 across equations
comments.quote: $q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})=\prod_{i=0}^{-(m-1)} q_{\theta}\left(H_{i-1} \mid H_{i}\right)$
comments.explanation: The passage defines the trial density as q_θ with subscript θ, but in para 285, the corresponding terms in the denominator use q_0 (with subscript 0). This creates ambiguity: is q_0 the same as q_θ, or is there a distinction (e.g., q_0 being the proposal at some reference parameter value)? The text in para 283 mentions 'trial densities q_0(H)' but the displayed equation uses q_θ. This notation switch between the main formula and its application in the weight calculation could confuse readers about whether θ is being varied in the proposal distribution or held fixed.

comments.title: Typo in recursive weight formula: incorrect conditioning index
comments.quote: w_{-(t-1)} \frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right)}
comments.explanation: Looking at the recursive decomposition of the weight, the numerator should involve the ratio of one additional p-term to one additional q-term when going from w_{-(t-1)} to w_{-t}. The denominator correctly adds q_θ(H_{-t} | H_{-(t-1)}), which is the new proposal step. However, the numerator shows p_θ(H_{-(t-1)} | H_{-1}), conditioning on H_{-1}, which is inconsistent with the Markov structure. It should be p_θ(H_{-(t-1)} | H_{-t}), conditioning on H_{-t}, to match the pattern in the full product form shown on the left side of the equation. The conditioning index H_{-1} appears to be a typo for H_{-t}. This is a subscript error that would confuse readers attempting to implement the recursion.

comments.title: Inconsistent subscript convention in proposal density
comments.quote: q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right) \ldots q_{0}\left(H_{-1} \mid H_{0}\right)
comments.explanation: In para 284, the trial density is defined as q_θ(H_{i-1} | H_i), meaning we condition on the 'later' state (larger index, closer to time 0) to generate the 'earlier' state (smaller index, going back in time). Following this convention with negative indices, q_θ(H_{-t} | H_{-(t-1)}) would mean conditioning on H_{-(t-1)} (which is closer to 0) to generate H_{-t} (further back). This is correct and consistent with the coalescent going backward in time. However, I initially found the ordering confusing because the denominator is written from the earliest time (-t) to the latest (-1), while the numerator p-terms go the opposite direction. Upon reflection, this is actually consistent since both are products over all steps—the ordering within the product doesn't matter mathematically. The real issue is the H_{-1} typo noted above, not the ordering convention.

comments.title: Inconsistent subscript in recursive weight formula carries into final weight
comments.quote: $w=w_{-m} p_{\theta}\left(H_{-m}\right) p_{\theta}\left(\left|H_{1}\right|=n+1\right)$
comments.explanation: Looking at this formula, I notice it references $|H_1|=n+1$, but throughout the paper the indexing convention uses negative indices for the coalescent history (going backward in time from $H_0$ to $H_{-m}$). The term $H_1$ with a positive subscript appears nowhere else in the context and seems inconsistent with the established notation. I suspect this should be $|H_{-m}|=1$ (indicating coalescence to a single lineage) or possibly $|H_0|=n$ (the initial sample size). The condition $n+1$ also seems unusual—typically we'd expect the MRCA to have size 1, not $n+1$. This appears to be either a transcription error or an undefined notational convention that could mislead readers trying to implement the method.

comments.title: Inconsistency between trial density subscript and weight formula
comments.quote: q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right)
comments.explanation: In para 284, the trial density is defined with subscript $\theta$ as $q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$, yet in the weight formula in para 285 (and implicitly used in the passage), the authors switch to $q_0$ without explanation. This could represent either: (1) a specific choice $\theta=0$, (2) an initial/proposal distribution distinct from the target, or (3) a notational error. While importance sampling commonly uses different subscripts to distinguish proposal from target distributions, the sudden switch from $q_\theta$ to $q_0$ without clarification could confuse readers about whether these represent the same object or different ones.

comments.title: Resampling weight assignment underspecified
comments.quote: The weight for each new sample is equal to the sample average of the $w_{-t}^{(j)}$.
comments.explanation: The authors state that after resampling, each new sample receives a weight equal to the sample average of the original weights. While this is a standard approach in sequential Monte Carlo (sometimes called the 'unbiased' resampling scheme), the description is ambiguous about a key detail: should this be the arithmetic mean $\bar{w}_{-t} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_j w_{-t}^{(j)}$, or should it account for the resampling multiplicities? Additionally, it's not stated how these reset weights should be propagated forward—whether subsequent weight updates multiply this average or restart accumulation. For reproducibility, the authors should clarify whether the weights are fully reset to $\bar{w}$ or whether some normalized form is used.

comments.title: Apparent error in factorization of joint density
comments.quote: \int p\left(Y_{\mathrm{obs}} \mid \theta\right) p\left(Y_{\mathrm{mis}} \mid Y_{\mathrm{obs}}, \theta\right) \mu\left(\mathrm{d} Y_{\mathrm{mis}}\right)
comments.explanation: Initially, I was checking whether the factorization p(Y_mis, Y_obs | θ) = p(Y_obs | θ) p(Y_mis | Y_obs, θ) is correct. This factorization is indeed valid by the chain rule of probability. However, the issue is with the integral: since p(Y_obs | θ) does not depend on Y_mis, it can be pulled outside the integral. The remaining integral ∫ p(Y_mis | Y_obs, θ) μ(dY_mis) equals 1 (as it's integrating a conditional density over its entire support). This would give L(θ | Y_obs) = p(Y_obs | θ), which is correct and consistent with the left-hand side. So mathematically the equation holds. However, looking at paragraph 315, the author claims this identity shows that 'L(θ | Y_obs) is the normalizing constant of p(Y_mis | Y_obs, θ)'. This is incorrect: L(θ | Y_obs) = p(Y_obs | θ) is the normalizing constant that makes p(Y_mis, Y_obs | θ)/p(Y_obs | θ) integrate to 1 over Y_mis, but the way the equation is written in the passage, the second integral is simply p(Y_obs | θ) × 1, which while true, doesn't directly illuminate the normalizing constant interpretation without additional explanation. The equation as stated is technically correct but the pedagogical connection to point (b) requires recognizing that p(Y_mis | Y_obs, θ) = p(Y_mis, Y_obs | θ)/L(θ | Y_obs), which isn't the factorization shown.

comments.title: Potentially overconfident claim about improvements
comments.quote: give me reasons to speculate that they can offer non-trivial improvements over the methods compared in this timely paper
comments.explanation: The author cites comparative studies (Meng and Schilling 1996, DiCiccio et al. 1997, Jensen and Kong 1999) as evidence that bridge sampling and path sampling methods can offer 'non-trivial improvements.' However, this claim is qualified only as speculation, and the cited studies may have been conducted in different contexts (not specifically coalescent or genealogical inference problems). The author does acknowledge this is speculation, which provides some hedging, but a careful reader might wonder whether these improvements would actually materialize in the specific high-dimensional latent variable structure of genealogical trees, where the performance characteristics could differ substantially from the settings in the cited papers. This is borderline acceptable given the explicit use of 'speculate,' but the phrase 'non-trivial improvements' may still overstate what can be inferred from studies in potentially different domains.

comments.title: Potentially misleading claim about 'perfect' importance sampling
comments.quote: A perfect importance sampling (IS) estimate of equation (4) is obtained using the sampler $Q_{\theta}^{*}(\mathcal{H}) \propto \pi_{\theta}\left(A_{n} \mid \mathcal{H}\right) P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$
comments.explanation: The term 'perfect' importance sampling typically refers to achieving zero-variance estimation. While the author correctly identifies the optimal proposal distribution (proportional to the target times the likelihood), calling the resulting estimate 'perfect' without qualification could be misleading. The zero-variance property holds specifically when estimating the normalizing constant, but for other functionals the variance reduction depends on the estimand. The Kahn and Marshall (1953) citation does support this as the optimal proposal for normalizing constant estimation, so the mathematical content is correct. However, a careful reader might wonder whether 'perfect' overstates what is achieved, especially since the subsequent sentence acknowledges this paper only provides an 'approximation' to this ideal sampler. This is a minor concern given the discussant format.

comments.title: Underspecified decomposition of variance and expectation
comments.quote: E\left\{l_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})\right\}=a+d, \\ \operatorname{var}\left\{l_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})\right\}=b+c
comments.explanation: The author introduces the decomposition where the conditional expectation on set A is 'a' and the unconditional expectation is 'a+d', and similarly for variance with 'b' conditional and 'b+c' unconditional. While this is intuitively meant to capture the contribution from the rare set A^c, the variance decomposition is stated without justification. By the law of total variance, Var(X) = E[Var(X|indicator)] + Var(E[X|indicator]), which doesn't generally yield a simple additive form 'b+c' where b is the conditional variance on A. The notation suggests that 'c' captures all the additional variance from the rare events, but this conflates within-group and between-group variance components. A careful reader might wonder whether this is an approximation (valid when P(A^c) is small) or an exact identity. Given this is a discussant's comment in a statistics paper where such decompositions are standard, the lack of even a brief justification for why variance decomposes as 'b+c' rather than through the proper total variance formula could mislead readers about the mathematical structure of the pseudobias phenomenon.
