comments.quote: Analogy with proposition 2 then suggests choosing the most recent event backwards in time by drawing a chromosome uniformly at random from those satisfying either (a) or (b). This procedure defines an IS function $Q^{\mathrm{SD}}$ which we note is independent of $\theta$, removing the need to specify a driving value.
comments.explanation: The authors describe their proposal distribution for the infinite sites model by analogy with Proposition 2, but the specification is incomplete. In the finite-type case, Proposition 2 involves not just selecting which chromosome is involved, but also determining whether the event is a mutation or coalescence with specific probabilities depending on θ and the conditional sampling probabilities (equation 25 or the sampling scheme in part (c)). Here, the authors only describe uniformly sampling a chromosome satisfying condition (a) or (b), but do not specify: (1) how to handle the relative weighting between chromosomes satisfying (a) versus (b), (2) whether the uniform sampling is over chromosomes or over eligible events, and (3) how to compute the importance weights without specifying the exact proposal probabilities. The claim that the procedure is 'independent of θ' is surprising given that the optimal backward transitions in Theorem 1 depend on θ. A reader cannot reproduce this method from the description given.

comments.title: Ambiguity in what likelihood is being estimated for infinite sites
comments.quote: The likelihood for the unrooted gene tree is estimated, as in equation (9), by the average of the importance weights.
comments.explanation: Throughout the paper, the likelihood L(θ) = π_θ(A_n) is defined as the probability of the observed genetic types as a function of θ. However, for the infinite sites model, the authors state they estimate 'the likelihood for the unrooted gene tree' rather than the likelihood of the sample data. The unrooted gene tree is a deterministic function of the data under the infinite sites model (as explained in the same paragraph), so this may be equivalent, but the shift in language is potentially confusing. Furthermore, equation (9) estimates L(θ) for a specific θ, but the authors claim Q^{SD} is independent of θ here. It is unclear whether they mean the likelihood surface can be estimated from a single set of samples (as with a driving value) or whether something different is being computed. The relationship between this θ-independent proposal and the estimation of L(θ) as a function of θ needs clarification.

comments.title: Underspecified modification for rooted tree analysis
comments.quote: We modified our IS function to analyse rooted trees, by adding to conditions (a) and (b) above a condition that no mutation can occur backwards in time from the type of the MRCA.
comments.explanation: The authors mention modifying their IS function by adding a condition to 'conditions (a) and (b) above,' but these conditions are not present in the provided passage context. More importantly, the modification itself—preventing mutations 'backwards in time from the type of the MRCA'—is described only in this single sentence. For a reader attempting to reproduce or implement this modified sampler, critical details are missing: How is this constraint enforced in the sampling procedure? Does it modify the backward transition probabilities in equation (25)? How are the weights recalculated? The authors acknowledge the sampler 'does not take full account of the information contained in the position of the root' but do not explain what information is used or how. This underspecification is particularly problematic given that the comparison in Figure 7 relies on this modified method.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to optimal proposal distribution formula
comments.quote:
The optimal proposal distribution is then given by

$$
Q_{\theta}^{*}(\mathcal{H}) \propto P_{\theta}\left(\mathcal{H} \mid A_{n}\right) P\{O \mid r(\mathcal{H})\}
$$
comments.explanation: This formula for the optimal proposal distribution when outgroup data O is available differs from the earlier optimal proposal distribution discussed in the paper (which was simply proportional to the posterior P_θ(H|A_n)). The authors state this is optimal 'when P_θ(O|H) depends only on the root r(H),' but they don't derive or justify why this particular form is optimal. While the intuition makes sense—reweighting by the likelihood of outgroup data given the root—this is presented as if obvious. A reader might wonder whether this truly minimizes variance of the IS estimator for P_θ(A_n, O), or whether there's an implicit assumption about independence between A_n and O given the history that should be stated explicitly.

comments.title: Vague claim about relative likelihood estimation accuracy
comments.quote: Further, our experience is that, even when the estimate of the likelihood surface is poor, the corresponding estimate of the relative likelihood surface is often accurate. This might be explained by the observation (R. C. Griffiths, personal communication) that, as a function of $\theta$, the shape of $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ normalized by its maximum seems to be similar for many $\mathcal{H}$.
comments.explanation: The authors make an empirical claim that relative likelihood surfaces remain accurate even when absolute likelihood estimates are poor, attributing this to a personal communication suggesting P_θ(H) has similar normalized shapes across different histories. However, this explanation is speculative ('might be explained') and relies on an informal observation rather than any theoretical or rigorous empirical analysis. Given that this property would be practically important for practitioners, the claim would benefit from either theoretical justification or systematic empirical verification rather than anecdotal experience and personal communication.

comments.title: Vague reference to 'more than one IS function' combination without specifying how
comments.quote: we note that methods which combine the results of more than one IS function, such as bridge sampling (see for example Gelman and Meng (1998)) would produce more reliable results.
comments.explanation: The authors suggest that combining multiple IS functions via bridge sampling would improve results, but they do not specify how this would be implemented in their specific context. Bridge sampling typically requires careful design of the bridge function and assumptions about the overlap between distributions. Given that the paper introduces a novel IS framework for population genetics, a reader attempting to implement this suggestion would need more guidance on how to choose multiple driving values, how to combine the resulting estimates, and what computational overhead this entails. This is particularly relevant since the authors note that designing even a single efficient IS function 'may be extremely challenging.'

comments.title: Claim about MCMC methods II mixing better lacks supporting evidence
comments.quote: Further, we believe that MCMC methods II (see Section 3) which move around the parameter space (i.e. that allow $\theta$ to vary in the cases that we consider here) will tend to mix better over tree space and are an efficient method of performing either Bayesian or likelihood inference for the parameters
comments.explanation: The authors state a belief about MCMC methods II having better mixing properties, but this claim is not supported by any theoretical argument or empirical evidence in the passage or referenced sections. The comparison throughout Section 5 focuses on MCMC methods I (fixed θ₀) versus their IS approach, with no direct empirical comparison to MCMC methods II. The phrase 'we believe' acknowledges uncertainty, but for a methodological paper making recommendations, this claim about relative efficiency should either be demonstrated or more clearly flagged as conjecture. A reader might incorrectly take this as an established finding.

comments.title: Underspecified procedure for extending IS to ancestral inference
comments.quote: For the simple demographic models considered here, the conditional distribution of the full typed ancestry $\mathcal{A}$ of the sample given $\mathcal{H}$ is easy to find, and it is straightforward to perform inference for all aspects of the typed ancestry relating the sample
comments.explanation: The authors claim that computing P(𝒜|ℋ) is 'easy' and ancestral inference is 'straightforward,' but provide no details on how this is actually done. The definition in the summary establishes that 𝒜 includes time and topology information while ℋ excludes it. Recovering the distribution of times and topology given only the history ℋ involves integrating over coalescent times conditional on the mutation ordering, which is not trivial to implement correctly. The reference to Stephens (2000) for 'more details' is helpful but insufficient for a reader trying to reproduce the method from this paper alone.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about detecting skewed weight distributions
comments.quote: IS appears to have a slight advantage here: although highly skewed distributions for the weights may cause problems, detecting this pitfall once we are aware of it appears to be an easier problem (in most contexts) than monitoring the convergence of a Markov chain in such a high dimensional space.
comments.explanation: This claim that detecting skewed IS weights is easier than monitoring MCMC convergence is immediately contradicted by the authors' own parenthetical admission: 'It is, however, not difficult to contrive situations where the distribution of the weights is such that it would be impossible to detect any problems just by looking at the observed weights.' The paper itself demonstrates in Table 1 (θ=15.0) that standard errors can severely underestimate true uncertainty when weights are highly skewed. The qualifier 'in most contexts' is vague and unsupported, potentially misleading practitioners about the reliability of IS diagnostics.

comments.title: Underspecified method for adapting IS scheme to handle ascertainment bias
comments.quote: For the IS schemes that we have considered, assuming the infinite sites mutation model, the ascertainment effect can be accommodated by labelling every lineage which leads to any chromosome in the panel as a panel lineage and adapting both $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ and $Q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ so that mutations can occur only on panel lineages.
comments.explanation: The authors describe a modification to handle SNP ascertainment bias by restricting mutations to 'panel lineages.' However, this description is too vague for implementation. Several key details are missing: (1) How exactly should the backward transition probabilities be modified when constructing $Q_\theta(\mathcal{H})$? (2) What happens when a coalescence event occurs between a panel and non-panel lineage—does the merged lineage inherit panel status? (3) How should the importance weights be computed given these constraints? (4) The statement that mutations 'can occur only on panel lineages' seems to describe the forward-in-time generative model under ascertainment, but the connection to the backward-in-time IS scheme is not made explicit. A reader trying to implement this would face significant ambiguity about the correct procedure.

comments.title: Claim about variance finiteness lacks sufficient justification
comments.quote: Although analytical results may sometimes be available, this is often not the case. For example, we could not prove finiteness of the variance of our weights, except in the special case of the infinite sites model where the number of possible histories is finite.
comments.explanation: The authors state they could not prove finite variance of their importance weights except for infinite sites. This is an important caveat since infinite variance would make standard error estimates meaningless and the CLT inapplicable. However, the empirical results throughout the paper rely heavily on standard errors computed from sample variance. The authors don't adequately address whether the good empirical performance of $Q_\theta^{\mathrm{SD}}$ (compared to $Q_\theta^{\mathrm{GT}}$) might partly be due to the variance being finite for their proposal but not for Griffiths-Tavaré's. Additionally, the claim about infinite sites having finite histories is stated without explanation—while it's true the number of distinct unrooted gene trees is finite, the number of possible orderings/histories leading to them could still be large, and the argument deserves elaboration.

comments.title: Generalized Pareto approach described without key implementation details
comments.quote: In particular we propose fitting a generalized Pareto distribution (see Davison and Smith (1990) for example) to the weights above some threshold, and using a parametric bootstrap to estimate confidence intervals for the mean of the weights (i.e. the estimate of the likelihood).
comments.explanation: The authors propose using extreme value theory to handle skewed weight distributions, which is a reasonable idea. However, critical implementation details are missing: (1) How should the threshold be selected? This is a notoriously difficult problem in extreme value statistics with significant impact on results. (2) What parametric bootstrap procedure is intended—resampling from the fitted GPD for the tail and empirical distribution below, or something else? (3) How should the mean be estimated when combining the body and tail of the distribution? The phrase 'although this procedure may be inexact' acknowledges limitations but doesn't help a reader implement the method. Given this is presented as a proposed diagnostic procedure, more specificity would be needed for reproducibility.

comments.title: Discrepancy in geometric distribution parameter between text and formula
comments.quote: where $m$ is geometrically distributed with parameter $l \theta /(n+l \theta)$
comments.explanation: According to Definition 1 in the paper summary, the approximation $\hat{\pi}(\cdot \mid A_n)$ involves mutating a uniformly chosen chromosome a geometric number of times with parameter $\theta/(n+\theta)$. Here with $l$ loci each mutating at rate $\theta/2$, the total mutation rate is $l\theta/2$, so effectively $\theta_{\text{total}} = l\theta$. Substituting into the original formula $\theta/(n+\theta)$ would give $l\theta/(n+l\theta)$, which matches what's written. However, this interpretation requires careful verification: if each locus mutates independently at rate $\theta/2$, and mutations are applied sequentially, the geometric parameter should reflect the probability of 'no more mutations.' The authors' claim that this is 'equivalent to drawing a time $t$ from an exponential distribution with rate parameter 1' needs the rate to be $(n + l\theta)/l\theta$ for the geometric, which corresponds to success probability $l\theta/(n+l\theta)$. This appears consistent, though the connection between the discrete geometric model and the continuous-time Poisson representation deserves more explicit justification for readers to verify the equivalence.

comments.title: Missing specification of Gaussian quadrature type and domain transformation
comments.quote: The integral in equation (31) may be approximated by using Gaussian quadrature (see for example Evans (1993)): $\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid A_{n}\right)=\sum_{\alpha \in A_{n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s} \frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} w_{i} F_{\alpha_{1} \beta_{1}}^{\left(\theta, t_{i}, n\right)} \ldots F_{\alpha_{l} \beta_{l}}^{\left(\theta, t_{i}, n\right)}$
comments.explanation: The integral in equation (31) is over the semi-infinite domain $[0,\infty)$ with an exponential weight $\exp(-t)$. Standard Gaussian quadrature formulas (e.g., Gauss-Legendre) apply to finite intervals, so applying them here requires either Gauss-Laguerre quadrature (designed for $\int_0^\infty e^{-t} f(t) dt$) or a domain transformation. The authors do not specify which type of quadrature they use, nor do they describe how the semi-infinite domain is handled. The statement 'We used $s=4$ quadrature points' is insufficient for reproducibility—a reader cannot determine the quadrature points $t_1, \ldots, t_4$ and weights $w_1, \ldots, w_4$ without knowing the quadrature rule employed. This is an underspecified method that prevents exact reproduction of their numerical scheme.

comments.title: Truncation criterion for infinite sum in equation (32) unspecified
comments.quote: The matrices $F^{\left(\theta, t_{i}, n\right)}$ given by equation (32) may each be approximated by a finite sum with a large number of terms (these matrices need only be found once for any particular problem).
comments.explanation: Equation (32) defines $F_{\alpha_i \beta_i}^{(\theta, t, n)}$ as an infinite sum over $m$. The authors state this 'may be approximated by a finite sum with a large number of terms' but provide no guidance on how many terms constitute 'a large number,' nor what truncation criterion or error tolerance they used. Since the Poisson terms decay exponentially for large $m$, a reasonable truncation is straightforward in principle, but without specifying the criterion (e.g., truncate when terms fall below $10^{-10}$, or include terms up to $m = \mu + k\sigma$ for some $k$), the method cannot be precisely reproduced. This is particularly relevant since the quality of the IS function depends on how well $\hat{\pi}$ is approximated.

comments.title: Justification gap for equivalence between geometric mutation count and Poisson process
comments.quote: It follows from elementary properties of Poisson processes that this is equivalent to drawing a time $t$ from an exponential distribution with rate parameter 1 , and then applying $m_{i}$ mutations to locus $i(i=1, \ldots, l)$, where the $m_{i}$ are independent and Poisson distributed with mean $\theta t / n$
comments.explanation: The authors claim an equivalence between (1) applying a geometric number of mutations with parameter $l\theta/(n+l\theta)$, where each mutation picks a locus uniformly and applies transition $P$, and (2) drawing exponential time $t$ and applying independent Poisson($\theta t/n$) mutations at each locus. While this equivalence is plausible through a continuous-time embedding argument, the derivation is non-trivial and the phrase 'elementary properties of Poisson processes' understates the reasoning required. Specifically, one must show that conditioning on the total number of mutations being geometric with the stated parameter, and distributing them uniformly across loci, yields the same marginal distribution as the Poisson process formulation. A brief sketch or reference would help readers verify this crucial step.

comments.title: Incorrect volume number for Liu et al. (1999) reference
comments.quote: Liu, J. S., Chen, R. and Wong, W. H. (1999) Rejection control and sequential importance sampling. J. Am. Statist. Ass., 93, 1022-1031.
comments.explanation: The paper summary mentions 'Liu et al., 1999' for rejection control, and in section 10 it is cited in the context of computational techniques. However, the reference lists the year as 1999 but gives volume 93. JASA volume 93 corresponds to 1998, not 1999. If the paper was published in 1999, the volume should be 94. This is a bibliographic inconsistency that could cause confusion for readers trying to locate the reference. This appears to be a minor error, but it is a verifiable numerical inconsistency in the published metadata.

comments.title: Missing author names in repeated reference entries
comments.quote:
(1998) Maximum likelihood estimation of population growth rates based on the coalescent. Genetics, 149, 429-434.
$\_\_\_\_$ (1999) Recombine. Computer Program.
comments.explanation: The reference list uses '$\_\_\_\_$' (underscores rendered as blanks) to indicate 'same authors as above' for Kuhner, Yamato, and Felsenstein entries. While this is a common bibliographic convention, it creates ambiguity because the 1998 entry also appears to use this convention following the 1995 entry. A reader might be uncertain whether these continuation marks are correctly applied, especially given that the 1995 entry clearly lists all three authors. The formatting is inconsistent with standard practice where the first entry in a sequence should always have full author names.

comments.title: Incomplete reference for Slade (2000)
comments.quote: Slade, P. (2000) Simulation of selected genealogies. Theor. Popln Biol., to be published.
comments.explanation: The reference indicates 'to be published' without providing volume or page numbers. While this is understandable for a paper at the time of writing, the paper summary indicates that Slade (2000) is cited in section 11 for the ancestral selection graph, suggesting it contains important methodological content. The incomplete bibliographic information makes it difficult for readers to verify the claims made about this work. This is a minor issue but represents underspecified sourcing for a key methodological reference.

comments.title: Underspecified procedure for approximating likelihood from MCMC samples
comments.quote: Thus an approximation to $L(\theta)$ that utilizes the summation over (distinct) $\mathcal{H}$ s visited by the Markov chain is available; such an approximation would be valid for all $\theta$, and thus gives a complete summary of $L(\theta)$.
comments.explanation: D.A. Stephens proposes that one could approximate L(θ) by summing over the distinct histories H visited by a converged MCMC chain targeting the posterior p(θ,H|A_n). However, this suggestion is underspecified in a way that could mislead readers. The likelihood L(θ) = ∫ p(A_n|θ,H)p(H|θ)dH involves an integral over the continuous (or very high-dimensional discrete) space of histories, weighted by the prior p(H|θ). Simply summing over visited H values without specifying how to account for (1) the density of visits approximating posterior probability rather than prior probability, (2) the change in p(H|θ) as θ varies, and (3) the normalizing constant issues, does not yield a valid estimator. The discussant acknowledges uncertainty ('Do the authors have any feeling for how good or poor such an approximation might be?'), but the framing suggests this is a straightforward procedure when in fact it conflates samples from the posterior with an integral under the prior, which requires careful reweighting (e.g., via Rao-Blackwellization or harmonic mean estimators, each with known pitfalls).

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about MCMC avoiding driving value difficulties
comments.quote: Is it valid to state that an MCMC scheme operating on the full posterior avoids this difficulty in any case?
comments.explanation: The discussant asks whether MCMC on the full posterior p(θ,H|A_n) avoids the problems associated with using a driving value θ_0 in IS schemes. This question is posed as if the answer might be straightforwardly affirmative, but this could mislead readers. While MCMC on the joint posterior does adapt to the current θ value when proposing H updates, it does not automatically avoid all difficulties: (1) if the proposal for H is based on a fixed approximate distribution (like Q_θ), efficiency may still degrade for θ far from where the approximation was tuned; (2) the joint chain must still mix well across both θ and H, which can be challenging when these are strongly coupled. The main paper (Section 6.1) notes that 'MCMC methods which fix θ at a driving value... appear to make things unnecessarily difficult for themselves,' but this does not imply that MCMC on the joint posterior is immune to related issues. The discussant's framing could lead readers to overestimate the robustness of joint MCMC schemes.

comments.title: Potential sign error in recursion equation (34)
comments.quote: $p(\mathbf{n})=\frac{\theta}{n+\theta-1}\left\{\sum_{\alpha=1}^{d} \sum_{\beta=1}^{d} \frac{n_{\beta}+1-\delta_{\alpha \beta}}{n} p_{\beta \alpha} p\left(\mathbf{n}+\mathbf{e}_{\beta}-\mathbf{e}_{\alpha}\right)\right\}+\frac{n-1}{n+\theta-1} \sum_{\left\{\alpha: n_{\alpha}>0\right\}} \frac{n_{\alpha}-1}{n-1} p\left(\mathbf{n}-\mathbf{e}_{\alpha}\right)$
comments.explanation: In Bob Griffiths's discussion, equation (34) presents a recursion for the sample probability. The mutation term involves $p(\mathbf{n}+\mathbf{e}_{\beta}-\mathbf{e}_{\alpha})$, which represents adding a type-$\beta$ individual and removing a type-$\alpha$ individual. However, when considering the first event backward in time under the coalescent, a mutation event that changes a type-$\alpha$ to type-$\beta$ going forward means that going backward we have one fewer type-$\beta$ and one more type-$\alpha$ in the ancestral configuration. The standard Griffiths-Tavaré recursion typically has $p(\mathbf{n}-\mathbf{e}_{\beta}+\mathbf{e}_{\alpha})$ for the mutation term (removing the mutant type $\beta$ and restoring the parental type $\alpha$). The expression $p(\mathbf{n}+\mathbf{e}_{\beta}-\mathbf{e}_{\alpha})$ appears to have the signs reversed, which would change the direction of the recursion. This may be a transcription error in the discussion contribution, though without seeing the original Griffiths-Tavaré papers referenced, I cannot be certain whether this reflects a different notational convention.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing convention in history notation
comments.quote: In Stephens-Donnelly notation let $H_{j}$ be the configuration of types at step $j$ of the history, $j=0,-1$, $-2 \ldots$; then equation ( 34 ) has the form $p\left(H_{j}\right)=\sum P\left(H_{j} \mid H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right) p\left(H_{j-1}^{\prime}\right)$
comments.explanation: The paper summary defines history as $(H_{-m}, H_{-(m-1)}, \ldots, H_{1}, H_{0})$ where the process runs from $H_{-m}$ (MRCA) forward to $H_0$ (sample). In Griffiths's discussion, he writes $P(H_j \mid H_{j-1}')$, which would mean the probability of $H_j$ given $H_{j-1}'$. Since $j-1 < j$, this conditions on an earlier (more ancestral) state, which is the forward-in-time direction. However, the recursion equation (34) is derived by considering the 'first event back in the coalescent process,' which goes backward in time. The conditioning should be on a state closer to the present (larger index). The notation $P(H_j \mid H_{j-1}')$ seems inconsistent with backward-in-time reasoning where we would expect $P(H_{j-1} \mid H_j)$ or similar. This creates potential confusion about which direction the Markov transitions operate.

comments.title: Ambiguous specification of panel lineage modification for IS
comments.quote: the ascertainment effect can be accommodated by labelling every lineage which leads to any chromosome in the panel as a panel lineage and adapting both $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ and $Q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ so that mutations can occur only on panel lineages
comments.explanation: The authors describe how to handle ascertainment bias in SNP data by restricting mutations to panel lineages. However, in the backward-in-time IS framework, 'lineages leading to panel chromosomes' is not well-defined until the full history is generated—lineages merge at coalescence events, and whether a lineage is ancestral to a panel member depends on future coalescence events in the simulation. The paper does not specify how to operationally identify panel lineages during the sequential construction of histories, nor how to modify the proposal probabilities $Q_\theta$ accordingly. This makes the method difficult to implement without additional clarification on whether panel ancestry is tracked dynamically or determined post-hoc with appropriate weight corrections.

comments.title: Underspecified Gaussian quadrature implementation
comments.quote: The integral in equation (31) may be approximated by using Gaussian quadrature (see for example Evans (1993)): $\hat{\pi}\left(\beta \mid A_{n}\right)=\sum_{\alpha \in A_{n}} \sum_{i=1}^{s} \frac{n_{\alpha}}{n} w_{i} F_{\alpha_{1} \beta_{1}}^{\left(\theta, t_{i}, n\right)} \ldots F_{\alpha_{l} \beta_{l}}^{\left(\theta, t_{i}, n\right)}$
comments.explanation: The authors use Gaussian quadrature to approximate the integral $\int \exp(-t) \ldots dt$ over $[0,\infty)$. Standard Gaussian quadrature (e.g., Gauss-Legendre) is designed for finite intervals, while Gauss-Laguerre quadrature is appropriate for integrals of the form $\int_0^\infty e^{-t} f(t) dt$. The paper does not specify which quadrature scheme is used, what the quadrature points $t_i$ and weights $w_i$ are, or over what effective range the integration is performed. With only $s=4$ quadrature points mentioned, the choice of scheme significantly affects accuracy. A reader attempting to reproduce these results would need to guess the appropriate quadrature method, making replication difficult.

comments.title: Underspecified threshold selection for generalized Pareto distribution fitting
comments.quote: In particular we propose fitting a generalized Pareto distribution (see Davison and Smith (1990) for example) to the weights above some threshold, and using a parametric bootstrap to estimate confidence intervals for the mean of the weights
comments.explanation: The authors propose fitting a generalized Pareto distribution to importance weights exceeding 'some threshold' but provide no guidance on how this threshold should be chosen. In extreme value theory, threshold selection is a critical and non-trivial problem that significantly affects the quality of the fit and subsequent inference. Different threshold choices can lead to substantially different parameter estimates and confidence intervals. While the authors acknowledge the procedure 'may be inexact,' a reader attempting to implement this diagnostic would need some indication of how to select the threshold (e.g., mean residual life plots, parameter stability plots, or specific quantile recommendations). This underspecification makes the method difficult to reproduce reliably.

comments.title: Unspecified details for adapting IS proposal distribution under ascertainment
comments.quote: For the IS schemes that we have considered, assuming the infinite sites mutation model, the ascertainment effect can be accommodated by labelling every lineage which leads to any chromosome in the panel as a panel lineage and adapting both $P_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ and $Q_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ so that mutations can occur only on panel lineages.
comments.explanation: The authors describe adapting both the coalescent prior P_θ(H) and the proposal distribution Q_θ(H) to restrict mutations to panel lineages, but the specific modifications are not given. For P_θ(H), one needs to know how the probability of mutation events changes when conditioned on occurring only on panel lineages. For Q_θ(H), which was carefully constructed using approximations to conditional sampling probabilities, it is unclear how the backward transition probabilities should be modified. Since the proposal distribution Q_θ^SD was derived from specific approximations to π(·|A_n), adapting it for the ascertainment constraint is non-trivial and would require knowing whether one modifies the approximate conditional probabilities or takes a different approach entirely.

comments.title: Gaussian quadrature approximation quality left uncharacterized
comments.quote: Although in some cases the approximation to $\hat{\pi}(\cdot \mid \cdot)$ obtained through this procedure is rather rough, we note that in any case the IS function defined by this approximation to $\hat{\pi}(\cdot \mid \cdot)$ is a valid IS function in its own right, and so leads to an estimator (9) which is consistent.
comments.explanation: The authors acknowledge the approximation may be 'rather rough' but do not characterize when this occurs or what the consequences are for efficiency. While consistency is preserved (as they correctly note), a rough approximation to the optimal proposal can lead to highly variable importance weights and poor effective sample sizes. The choice of s=4 quadrature points is stated without justification, and no guidance is given on when more points might be needed or how to assess whether the approximation quality is adequate for a given problem. For practitioners, understanding the trade-off between computational cost and approximation quality would be valuable.

comments.title: Rao-Blackwellization formula missing prior specification
comments.quote: $\pi\left(\theta \mid \mathcal{H}^{i}, A_{n}\right)=\frac{T^{i}}{2} \frac{\left(T^{i} \theta / 2\right)^{m^{i}} \exp \left(-T^{i} \theta / 2\right)}{m^{i!}}$
comments.explanation: Beaumont presents this as the conditional posterior distribution of θ given the genealogy. However, the expression shown is proportional to a Gamma distribution in θ (specifically, a Gamma with shape m^i + 1 and rate T^i/2), which would arise from combining the Poisson likelihood for mutations with a specific prior. The text states 'Using a uniform (improper) prior' earlier, but with an improper uniform prior on θ ∈ (0,∞), the posterior conditional on H^i would be proportional to θ^{m^i} exp(-T^i θ/2), which is a Gamma(m^i + 1, T^i/2) distribution. The normalizing constant should be (T^i/2)^{m^i+1}/Γ(m^i + 1), not (T^i/2) × (T^i θ/2)^{m^i}/(m^i!). The factor T^i/2 outside appears inconsistent with the standard normalized form. Additionally, 'm^{i!}' appears to be a typo for 'm^i!' (factorial notation), but even correcting this, the dimensional analysis and normalization of this density formula are questionable.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about IS versus MCMC search space representation
comments.quote: In MCMC sampling, the missing data of the genealogy is represented as a topology with branch lengths. The size of the search space depends on the number of branches. Short sequences lead to many possible combinations of branch lengths and make a stable estimate difficult. In contrast, IS represents the missing data as a topology with mutations.
comments.explanation: Kuhner and Beerli claim that MCMC represents missing data as 'topology with branch lengths' while IS represents it as 'topology with mutations,' implying these are fundamentally different parameterizations. However, this distinction is somewhat misleading. Both approaches ultimately deal with the same genealogical structures; the difference is more about how the algorithms traverse the space rather than what they represent. The paper's earlier sections describe IS schemes that also involve genealogical trees with branch lengths (e.g., the scaled genealogical tree notation). The claim that 'the size of the search space depends on the number of branches' for MCMC but 'is determined by the number of mutations' for IS oversimplifies the relationship between sequence length, mutation count, and computational difficulty in ways that could mislead readers about the fundamental algorithmic differences.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in weight ratio formula
comments.quote: w_{-t}=\frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t}\right) \ldots p_{\theta}\left(H_{0} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right) \ldots q_{0}\left(H_{-1} \mid H_{0}\right)} \equiv w_{-(t-1)} \frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right)}
comments.explanation: The recursive relation on the right-hand side appears to contain an error. The term $p_{\theta}(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1})$ in the numerator of the incremental factor seems incorrect. Following the pattern established in the full product on the left, the incremental step from $w_{-(t-1)}$ to $w_{-t}$ should involve $p_{\theta}(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t})$ in the numerator (the transition probability for the newly added step), not $p_{\theta}(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1})$ which would condition on $H_{-1}$ rather than $H_{-t}$. This appears to be a typo where $H_{-t}$ was miswritten as $H_{-1}$.

comments.title: Underspecified resampling threshold criterion
comments.quote: A resampling step is incurred at time $-t$ when the coefficient of variation in $\left\{w_{-t}^{(1)}, \ldots, w_{-t}^{(M)}\right\}$ exceeds a threshold $B$.
comments.explanation: While the authors mention using $B=4$ in their experiments, the choice of this threshold is not justified, nor is guidance provided on how to select it in general. The coefficient of variation threshold directly affects the frequency of resampling steps and thus the efficiency-variance tradeoff. A reader attempting to implement this method would need to know whether $B=4$ is problem-specific or a general recommendation, and how sensitive results are to this choice. This is particularly important since the authors claim 'negligible' extra computational cost with only two resampling steps, but this depends critically on the threshold.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of modified resampling procedure
comments.quote: A modification that we propose is to resample among the same size samples $\left\{H_{-i_{1}}^{(1)}, \ldots, H_{-i_{M}}^{(M)}\right\}$, where $i_{j}=\min \left\{t:\left|H_{-t}^{(j)}\right|=i\right\}$.
comments.explanation: This proposed modification is underspecified. The notation suggests resampling among histories that have reached the same configuration size $i$, but several key details are missing: (1) How is the size $i$ chosen at which to perform resampling? (2) What happens to samples that have not yet reached size $i$ when some samples have? (3) How are weights adjusted when resampling at different time indices $i_j$ across samples? The standard resampling described earlier operates at a fixed time $-t$ across all samples, but this modification breaks that synchronization. Without these details, a reader cannot implement the proposed improvement.

comments.title: Underspecified adaptive scheme for choosing mixing parameter δ
comments.quote: An adaptive estimate of $\delta$ is then obtained by solving $h\left(\hat{Z}_{M}\right)=\hat{P}\left(\hat{Z}_{M} \mid X\right)$.
comments.explanation: The authors describe an adaptive scheme for choosing δ in their defensive mixture proposal distribution. They state that δ is obtained by solving h(Ẑ_M) = P̂(Ẑ_M | X), but this equation may not always have a valid solution in [0,1]. Given h(Z) = δP(Z) + (1-δ)P(Z|τ=τ̂,X), solving for δ gives δ = [P̂(Ẑ_M|X) - P(Ẑ_M|τ=τ̂,X)] / [P(Ẑ_M) - P(Ẑ_M|τ=τ̂,X)]. There is no discussion of what happens when the denominator is zero, when the resulting δ falls outside [0,1], or how to handle such cases. For reproducibility, boundary conditions and fallback procedures should be specified.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about infinite variance conditions
comments.quote: Stephens and Donnelly also point out that the variance of our importance sampling weights can become infinite when the trial value of $\theta$ used in the sample is smaller than the true value by more than a factor of 2 . It is important to note that this rule is for $n=2$.
comments.explanation: Felsenstein states that the factor-of-2 rule for infinite variance applies specifically to n=2, suggesting it may be more stringent for larger sample sizes. However, this is presented without mathematical justification or reference to where this sample-size dependence is derived. The claim that 'conditions for misbehaviour may be more stringent' for larger n could mislead readers about the safety of using misspecified θ₀ values, as the relationship between n and the infinite variance threshold is not established. The subsequent empirical demonstration with n=50 does not constitute a proof of this claim.

comments.title: Vague description of how to choose starting value θ₀
comments.quote: One criticism concerns the absence of any discussion on how to choose a starting value $\theta_{0}$. The authors acknowledge that their sample selection depends on $\theta_{0}$, and that simulating with a $\theta_{0}$ that is distant from the true maximum may yield a sample that excludes important histories.
comments.explanation: This is a comment from Bob Mau highlighting a methodological gap in the main paper. While this is a discussant's critique rather than an error in the passage itself, it points to an underspecified aspect of the authors' method. The passage from Mau correctly identifies that the driving value θ₀ is a crucial parameter choice that affects the quality of importance sampling estimates, yet the main paper apparently provides no guidance on how to select it. The discussant notes that the authors' examples happened to guess well, but this does not constitute a reproducible methodology. For practitioners trying to implement the method, this represents a significant gap—the algorithm cannot be fully reproduced without knowing how to choose this parameter.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about MCMC vs IS missing data representation
comments.quote: In MCMC sampling, the missing data of the genealogy is represented as a topology with branch lengths. The size of the search space depends on the number of branches. Short sequences lead to many possible combinations of branch lengths and make a stable estimate difficult. In contrast, IS represents the missing data as a topology with mutations.
comments.explanation: Kuhner and Beerli's characterization of the fundamental difference between MCMC and IS approaches is somewhat misleading. Both approaches ultimately work with genealogical histories, but the claim that 'short sequences lead to many possible combinations of branch lengths' conflates the continuous nature of branch lengths with the discrete mutation structure. The real issue is that with short sequences, there are few segregating sites to constrain the genealogy, making the posterior over branch lengths diffuse. The IS approach sidesteps this by conditioning on mutation counts, but the claim that the 'size of the search space depends on the number of branches' for MCMC versus 'number of mutations' for IS oversimplifies the relationship. Both methods must effectively integrate over genealogies consistent with the data; the difference is in how they parameterize and sample this space.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in importance weight ratio formula
comments.quote: w_{-t}=\frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t}\right) \ldots p_{\theta}\left(H_{0} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right) \ldots q_{0}\left(H_{-1} \mid H_{0}\right)} \equiv w_{-(t-1)} \frac{p_{\theta}\left(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1}\right)}{q_{0}\left(H_{-t} \mid H_{-(t-1)}\right)}
comments.explanation: The recursive update formula appears to contain an error. In the recursion on the right side, the numerator shows $p_{\theta}(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-1})$, but this should be $p_{\theta}(H_{-(t-1)} \mid H_{-t})$ to be consistent with the expanded product form on the left. The notation $H_{-1}$ in the conditional makes no sense in this context since we're updating from step $-(t-1)$ to $-t$, and the previous step involves $H_{-t}$ not $H_{-1}$. This appears to be a typo where $H_{-t}$ was incorrectly written as $H_{-1}$.

comments.title: Undefined complete-data score function in path sampling suggestion
comments.quote: evaluations of $\left\{U_{i}=S\left(\theta^{(i)} \mid Y_{\mathrm{mis}}^{(i)}, Y_{\mathrm{obs}}\right), i=1, \ldots, M\right\}$ where $S\left(\theta \mid Y_{\mathrm{mis}}, Y_{\mathrm{obs}}\right)$ is the complete-data score function
comments.explanation: The discussant (Meng) introduces the complete-data score function $S(\theta \mid Y_{\mathrm{mis}}, Y_{\mathrm{obs}})$ without providing its definition or explaining how it relates to the coalescent model framework used throughout the paper. While the concept of a score function is standard, the specific form for coalescent models with history $\mathcal{H}$ as the missing data is not obvious. For a reader to implement path sampling as suggested, they would need to know how to compute this score function for the genealogical histories, but this is not specified. This makes the proposed method underspecified for the specific application domain.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of set A in pseudobias discussion
comments.quote: and $A=\left\{\mathcal{H}: \omega_{\theta}(\mathcal{H}) \doteq 0\right\}$
comments.explanation: The discussant (Ventura) defines the set $A$ using the notation $\omega_{\theta}(\mathcal{H}) \doteq 0$, but the symbol $\omega_{\theta}$ is not defined anywhere in the passage. Just above, $w_{\theta}(\mathcal{H})$ (with lowercase w) is defined as the importance weight ratio. The use of $\omega$ (omega) instead of $w$ appears to be either a typo or introduces an undefined quantity. Furthermore, the notation $\doteq 0$ is non-standard and its precise meaning (approximately zero? asymptotically zero?) is not clarified, making the mathematical characterization of the pseudobias phenomenon ambiguous.

comments.title: Variance reduction formula inverted
comments.quote: Its asymptotic variance is $\left(1-\rho_{\theta}^{2}\right)^{-1}$ times smaller than that of expression (12)
comments.explanation: The standard result for control variates is that the variance is reduced by a factor of $(1-\rho^2)$, meaning the new variance equals $(1-\rho^2)$ times the original variance. Saying the variance is '$(1-\rho_{\theta}^{2})^{-1}$ times smaller' is confusing and likely incorrect. If the new variance is $(1-\rho^2)$ times the original, then it is $\rho^2/(1-\rho^2)$ times smaller, or equivalently the reduction factor is $(1-\rho^2)^{-1}$ for the precision (inverse variance), not the variance itself being '$(1-\rho^2)^{-1}$ times smaller.' The phrasing seems to conflate variance reduction with precision improvement. A clearer statement would be that the asymptotic variance is $(1-\rho_{\theta}^{2})$ times that of expression (12), or that the estimator is $(1-\rho_{\theta}^{2})^{-1}$ times more efficient.

comments.title: Inconsistent indexing in control variate definition
comments.quote: with $w_{k}(\mathcal{H})=Q_{k}(\mathcal{H}) / Q(\mathcal{H})$, and $\hat{\beta}_{\theta k}$ the estimates of $\beta_{\theta k}$ in the regression
comments.explanation: In expression (38), the control variate is defined as $w_{\theta}^{\prime}=Q_{\theta}^{\mathrm{SD}}(\mathcal{H}) / Q(\mathcal{H})$, which depends on $\theta$. However, in expression (39), the weights $w_k(\mathcal{H})=Q_k(\mathcal{H})/Q(\mathcal{H})$ use a fixed index $k$ corresponding to the mixture components (with $Q_k = Q_{\theta_k}^{\mathrm{SD}}$). The text claims expression (39) 'implicitly approximates expression (38)', but the connection is not made explicit. Since expression (38) uses $Q_{\theta}^{\mathrm{SD}}$ for the specific $\theta$ being evaluated, while expression (39) uses only the fixed mixture components $Q_k$, the approximation quality depends on how well the mixture spans the $\theta$ values of interest. This relationship is stated but the mechanism of approximation is underspecified.

comments.title: Denominator mismatch between expressions (38) and (39)
comments.quote: with $\hat{\beta}_{\theta}^{\prime}$ the least square regression slope of $l_{\theta}\left(\mathcal{H}^{(i)}\right)$ on $w_{\theta}^{\prime(i)}$
comments.explanation: In expression (38), the importance weights use $Q_{\theta_0}(\mathcal{H}^{(i)})$ in the denominator of the main term, but the control variate $w_{\theta}^{\prime}=Q_{\theta}^{\mathrm{SD}}(\mathcal{H})/Q(\mathcal{H})$ uses $Q(\mathcal{H})$ in its denominator. This means samples were drawn from $Q_{\theta_0}$ (presumably one of the mixture components or a single proposal), yet the control variate references the mixture $Q$. For the control variate method to work properly, the samples should be drawn from $Q$ as defined in equation (37), not from a single $Q_{\theta_0}$. The notation suggests either an error in the denominator of the main IS estimator (should be $Q$ not $Q_{\theta_0}$) or an inconsistency in how sampling is described.

comments.title: Variance result stated without sample size dependence but claimed to hold regardless of n
comments.quote: The result of Stephens (1999) can be extended to show that the variance of the IS weights for the Fluctuate scheme (Kuhner et al., 1998) is infinite for $\theta>2 \theta_{0}$, regardless of the value of the sample size $n$.
comments.explanation: Earlier in the paper summary, it states that 'the variance of our importance sampling weights can become infinite when the trial value of θ used in the sample is smaller than the true value by more than a factor of 2. It is important to note that this rule is for n=2. For more reasonable sample sizes the conditions for misbehaviour may be more stringent.' This suggests that the n=2 case is the least stringent, and larger sample sizes might require even closer θ₀ values to avoid infinite variance. The authors' claim that the variance is infinite for θ>2θ₀ 'regardless of the value of the sample size n' appears to contradict this earlier statement from Felsenstein's discussion. If the result truly holds regardless of n, this needs clarification of whether 'regardless of n' means the condition θ>2θ₀ is sufficient for all n (i.e., a universal sufficient condition), or whether the authors are making a stronger claim that contradicts the earlier discussion about conditions being 'more stringent' for larger n.

comments.title: Parenthetical criticism of Figure 11 lacks quantitative grounding
comments.quote: (Incidentally, the suggestion in Fig. 11 of differences by more than a factor of 2 in the estimated maximum likelihood estimates from different runs is somewhat disquieting.)
comments.explanation: The authors criticize Figure 11 for showing 'differences by more than a factor of 2 in the estimated maximum likelihood estimates from different runs.' However, looking at Figure 11's caption, the runs start from θ₀=0.0001 and converge toward θ=0.01 (the true value), representing a factor of 100 difference between initial and true values. Felsenstein's discussion accompanying the figure states 'Most of the variation in the results is presumably natural rather than due to a failure of convergence.' Without specifying which runs differ by more than a factor of 2, or whether this variation exceeds what would be expected from sampling variation alone, the criticism is somewhat vague. A more precise statement about which pairs of estimates differ and by how much would strengthen this critique.

comments.title: Incomplete reference entry for Ventura (1998)
comments.quote: - (1998) Nonparametric bootstrap recycling. Technical Report 673. Statistics Department, Carnegie Mellon University, Pittsburgh.
comments.explanation: The reference entry uses a dash to indicate the same author as the previous entry (Ventura, V.), which is a standard bibliographic convention. However, I initially wondered whether this might be a formatting error or incomplete entry. Checking the context, this appears to be a consistent pattern used throughout the references section (e.g., Kuhner et al. entries, Markovtsova et al. entries). The convention is clear and correctly applied here, so this is not actually an issue upon reflection.
